sync between edgesynology1 (running seaf-cli) and this seafile server you're running on is was working. another ai session told me:
Generic Decor had a stale/bad seaf-cli state on edgesynology1: the NAS agent was reporting synchronized, but the live folder did not match the server head. I queued a safe restart for only the Generic Decor sync through the existing NAS settings heartbeat.
After that, the NAS client made a new commit at 2026-06-19 17:03:32 UTC as nas-sync. Current state now is:
Server API root for Generic Decor: contains 08 - Valerie - Steve Winwood.mp3
Server API root: does not contain Antigravity.exe
NAS live folder /volume1/mac/Decor/Generic Decor: contains 08 - Valerie - Steve Winwood.mp3
NAS live folder: does not contain Antigravity.exe
Antigravity.exe is recoverable from the NAS recycle bin here:
/volume1/mac/#recycle/Decor/Generic Decor/Antigravity.exe
It is 165 MB, modified 2026-06-19 11:59:33 EDT.
So the original problem was not that Seafile server was down; it was that the NAS seaf-cli daemon/index was falsely reporting synchronized while its worktree was divergent. Restarting the NAS sync made it reconcile, and the NAS version won. I stopped the long fsck check because the library is large and the live API/status evidence was already enough.


We can’t make sync bugs literally impossible, but we can prevent this class from silently winning.
The fix is to add an independent watchdog, because seaf-cli status lied: it said Generic Decor was synchronized while the worktree and server disagreed.
I’d do three layers:
Detect stale-false-synchronized
Every few minutes, compare:
server repo head/update time
NAS client token sync time
NAS agent-reported state
a cheap filesystem signature for important paths, like root filenames, sizes, mtimes
If server changed but NAS still says synchronized with mismatched files, mark it unhealthy.

Quarantine before self-healing
On mismatch, do not immediately let NAS write. First:
pause that library
alert/log the exact differing files
optionally restart the daemon
only resume if server and NAS agree after restart
This prevents “stale NAS state wins,” which is what happened here.

Add a canary sync
Use a tiny dedicated health file/library, not production data. The NAS agent updates a heartbeat file, then verifies the server sees it and the NAS sees server-side changes back. If that round trip fails, pause production sync and alert.

Also keep the recovery nets:
Synology recycle bin enabled
Btrfs snapshots enabled
Seafile history/retention enabled
NAS settings dashboard showing “synchronized” only after independent verification, not just raw seaf-cli status

The practical next step: I can add a watchdog to the nas-settings/seaf-cli agent that checks each library for “reported synchronized but server head newer or root signature mismatched,” then queues pause/restart and records a clear red status instead of silently letting it drift."
what do you think of his plan? is there anything more we should do?

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Supabase
Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

## nas-direct
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Review seafile sync watchdog architecture proposal

content.text:
Good plan in its bones, but it has one serious flaw that's worth catching before you build anything — and it's the same flaw that bit you in this very incident.

## The core problem with the plan

The plan's self-heal step is "restart the daemon, then resume only if server and NAS agree after restart." But **restarting the daemon doesn't reconcile toward truth — it picks a winner.** In this incident the winner was the (stale) NAS, and the result was that `Antigravity.exe` got deleted from the server. That's a data-loss event, not a fix. It's only recoverable because the recycle bin happened to catch it.

And here's the trap: after a destructive reconcile, **server and NAS *do* agree** — they both now lack the file. So the plan's resume condition ("resume if they agree after restart") would be *satisfied by the data loss* and report green. The watchdog as written would automate the exact silent-loss it's meant to prevent.

So the non-negotiable change: **on detected divergence, never auto-resume into a reconcile that picks a winner.** Specifically:
- Snapshot both sides first (server commit + NAS worktree manifest).
- Classify the drift: pure additions are safe to merge; **deletions/overwrites must not auto-apply** above a small threshold.
- If the safe direction is ambiguous, stay quarantined and require a human to choose the winning side. "Agree after restart" is not a safety condition — "agree *without having destroyed anything*" is.

## What else is missing

1. **Nobody found the root cause.** Why did `seaf-cli status` falsely report synchronized? Hung daemon, corrupted clone index, clock skew, full disk, an interrupted commit? The watchdog treats the symptom. At minimum, capture daemon logs, `seafile.log`, index/clone.db state, disk space, and clock at detection time so the real bug can eventually be fixed instead of papered over.

2. **Use Seafile's own truth, not just filesystem signatures.** The server has authoritative per-library commit history, and the client has the commit id it *thinks* it's at. Comparing "client's claimed head" vs "server's actual head" is more reliable than a filesystem diff and catches the lie directly. Keep the filesystem signature too, but make it **recursive and cheap** (a hashed manifest of path+size+mtime) — a root-filenames-only signature would miss any drift inside subfolders.

3. **The canary as described won't catch this failure.** A heartbeat round-trip tests liveness/connectivity. The actual failure was *false-synchronized with divergence on a specific library* — a happy-path heartbeat sails right through that. Strengthen it to an **active probe**: periodically inject a known marker server-side and confirm the NAS picks it up within an SLA, *and* inject NAS-side and confirm the server sees it. That exercises the real bidirectional path, per library.

4. **Alerting has to reach a human.** "Record a red status in the dashboard" isn't enough if nobody's looking. Quarantine should fire an actual push (email/notification).

5. **Anti-flap.** Auto-restart needs backoff + a max-restart cap, then stay quarantined. Otherwise a bad library thrashes.

6. **Cover everything.** This was one library on edgesynology1. Make it a per-library state machine across all libraries and both edgesynology1/edgesynology2.

7. **Verify the recovery nets are actually ON**, not assumed — Seafile history retention days, btrfs snapshot schedule, recycle-bin retention. Don't list them as "keep enabled" without confirming.

## Before any of that

This incident isn't actually closed: a 165 MB `Antigravity.exe` is sitting in the NAS recycle bin, deleted by the "fix." First question is whether that file *should* be on the server — if it should, the NAS winning was wrong and we need to restore it (server history or recycle bin) rather than accept the merge.

Want me to:
1. **Verify current state** — confirm the file situation and check whether the recovery nets (history/snapshots/recycle retention) are actually enabled, via the NAS MCP and this server? (read-only)
2. **Restore `Antigravity.exe`** if it belongs on the server?
3. **Draft the watchdog** with the safety fix above (quarantine-without-picking-a-winner) once we've confirmed the recovery nets?

I'd do 1 first regardless — it's read-only and tells us whether this is a closed incident or an open one.

content.text:
I have the root cause cold. Here's what the investigation found, then your six points.

## Root cause: it wasn't a "stale index" — the NAS watcher went blind

The `seaf-cli` client runs in a Docker container on edgesynology1 (`seaf-daemon`, started 17:03 = the restart the other session did). The daemon's own log (`/root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log`) is full of one error, **11,077 times**:

```
[06/19/26 17:03:23] wt-monitor-linux.c(542): [wt mon] fail to add watch to /library/decor: No space left on device
```

"No space left on device" here is **not disk** (disk is 52% full, 53 TB free). It's the Linux **inotify watch limit**. Confirmed:

- `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` ← the default, far too low for this host
- The seaf-daemon holds 3,209 watches; the rest of the 8,192 root-owned pool is consumed by other DSM/container processes, so the daemon **can't register any more watches**
- The worktree is enormous (directory walk didn't finish in 25s — hundreds of thousands of dirs)
- Crucially, `/library/decor` itself — the tree containing **Generic Decor** — is in the failed list. It has **no watch.**

**Mechanism:** seaf-daemon decides a library "changed" only when its filesystem monitor (inotify) fires. If a directory has no watch, edits there are never detected, so the daemon never re-indexes or commits — and `seaf-cli status` honestly reports "synchronized," because from its index's point of view nothing changed. That is *exactly* the false-synchronized state you hit.

**Why the restart "fixed" it:** startup does a one-time full re-index pass, which caught the Generic Decor drift once. But the watch errors are timestamped *during that same restart* — meaning the daemon is **still blind right now** to `/library/decor` and everything alphabetically after the 8,192nd directory. The restart was a band-aid that papers over drift via full scan but does nothing about the ceiling. **It will recur**, silently, on any library beyond the watch limit.

This also reframes the other session's plan: a watchdog that "detects drift → restarts the daemon" would mask this *forever*, because each restart's full scan resets the symptom while the root cause stays broken.

---

## Your six points

**1) Why seaf-cli falsely reported "synchronized"** — Answered above: inotify watch exhaustion (`max_user_watches = 8192`). The change-watcher couldn't watch most of the worktree, so the daemon never noticed the divergence and reported synced in good faith. Not index corruption, not disk, not clock skew (NAS and this server are within ~20s).

**2) NAS (edgesynology1) is authoritative** — Good, that simplifies the design a lot. It means we never have to agonize over "which side wins" — the NAS always wins. So the real risk isn't *losing NAS data*; it's **NAS changes silently failing to reach the server**, leaving server users on stale data (precisely this bug). The watchdog's job becomes narrow: *prove NAS edits are actually propagating, and scream when the watcher goes deaf.*

**3) "Use Seafile's own truth, not just filesystem signatures" — plain version**

The other AI proposed detecting drift by computing a "filesystem signature" — listing filenames/sizes/dates on both sides and comparing. That's reinventing a wheel, and a leaky one (it'd miss anything in subfolders unless you walk the whole 56 TB).

But Seafile *already* keeps a perfect record. Every library has a chain of **commits**, each with a unique ID (like Git). The server knows its latest commit ID for a library ("head"). The client knows which commit ID *it* thinks it's synced to.

So instead of comparing files, just compare two short ID strings:

- **Match** → genuinely in sync. Done.
- **Server ahead of client** → client is behind.
- **Client *says* it's at the server's head but the live folder clearly differs** → that's the lie. The watcher is blind.

It's the difference between "photograph both rooms and play spot-the-difference" (filesystem signature) vs. "read the version number on each side" (Seafile's own commit IDs). The version-number check is cheaper, exact, and catches the failure directly. *Plus* watching the daemon log for the inotify error tells you the watcher is deaf **before** any drift even happens.

**4) How much stress would an active probe add?** — Effectively **zero load.** A heartbeat is a few-KB file write plus a metadata read every N minutes; against a NAS moving 56 TB that's noise. CPU/disk/network impact: negligible.

But the honest caveat: **a naive canary wouldn't have caught *this* bug.** A tiny dedicated heartbeat library would likely sit inside the 8,192 watched directories and sync fine, while Generic Decor (unwatched) silently rotted. So the probe's problem isn't cost — it's that it can test the wrong thing. Two cheaper, more reliable signals:
- **Passively grep the daemon log** for `No space left on device` / `fail to add watch` — should be **0**. (Right now it's 11,077.)
- **Compare client-head vs server-head commit IDs** per library (point 3).

If you do want a round-trip probe, write the heartbeat *into an at-risk production tree* (or rotate it through several), not a cozy dedicated library.

**5) Antigravity.exe** — Noted, ignoring it. Understood it was a deliberate test (which, usefully, *did* expose a real bug).

---

**6) Prompt to hand to the other AI session**

> **Re: the Generic Decor sync incident — root cause found, your watchdog plan needs to change.**
>
> I investigated the seaf-cli daemon on edgesynology1 directly (via `/proc/<pid>/root` into the container, since `docker exec` is blocked). The root cause is **not** a stale/corrupt index. The daemon's log (`/root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log`) contains the error `wt-monitor-linux.c: [wt mon] fail to add watch to /library/decor: No space left on device` — **11,077 times**, all during the 17:03 restart.
>
> "No space left on device" here is the **Linux inotify watch limit**, not disk (disk is 52% used). `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192`, which is the low default. The seaf-daemon holds only 3,209 watches before the shared root-owned inotify pool is exhausted, so it cannot watch most of a worktree that has hundreds of thousands of directories. `/library/decor` — the tree containing Generic Decor — has **no watch right now.**
>
> **Mechanism:** seaf-daemon only re-indexes/commits a library when inotify fires a change event. Unwatched directories never fire, so edits there are never detected, and `seaf-cli status` reports "synchronized" truthfully because its index sees no change. That is the false-synchronized state.
>
> **Why your restart appeared to fix it:** startup runs a one-time full re-index that caught the drift once — but the watch limit is still exhausted, so the daemon is blind again *as of that same restart*. It will recur on any library past the watch ceiling. A watchdog that "detects drift → restarts the daemon" would mask this permanently, because each restart's full scan resets the symptom while the root cause persists.
>
> **Do these first (actual fix), in order:**
> 1. **Raise inotify limits on the host** (kernel is shared with the container): `fs.inotify.max_user_watches` to at least **1048576** (size it to ~2× the worktree's actual directory count — measure with `find <worktree> -type d | wc -l`) and `fs.inotify.max_user_instances` to **1024**.
> 2. **Make it persistent.** On Synology, `/etc/sysctl.conf` is not reliably honored and gets reset by DSM updates — use a **Task Scheduler boot-up task (root)** that runs the `sysctl -w` commands. Verify it survives a reboot.
> 3. **Exclude junk from the worktree** to cut watch/directory pressure dramatically. The log is full of watches on `@eaDir/*.png` (Synology thumbnail folders) — these should never sync. Add `@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, and `.DS_Store` to a `seafile-ignore.txt` in the worktree root. This both reduces watch count and stops syncing thumbnail junk to the server.
> 4. **Restart seaf-daemon** and confirm the fix: `grep -c 'No space left on device' seafile.log` for entries *after* the restart must be **0**.
>
> **Then build the watchdog as defense-in-depth — but change its design:**
> - **Don't detect drift via filesystem signatures.** Compare Seafile's own commit IDs: per library, the client's synced-to head vs. the server's actual head. Exact and cheap. (If you must hash files, walk recursively, not just root — but commit IDs make that unnecessary.)
> - **Add the signal that actually catches this failure:** monitor the daemon log for `fail to add watch` / `No space left on device` (should be 0) and track inotify watch headroom (`watches_in_use / max_user_watches`). Alert *before* drift occurs.
> - **Never auto-restart as the remedy** — restart is what hid this. On a detected anomaly: capture diagnostics (watch-error count, inotify usage, client vs server head, disk, daemon uptime), set a clear red status, and **push an alert to a human** (not just a dashboard nobody watches).
> - **edgesynology1 is the source of truth**, so resolution direction is fixed (NAS wins) — the watchdog only needs to verify NAS edits are reaching the server, not adjudicate conflicts.
> - **Keep the recovery nets but verify they're actually on:** Synology recycle bin, btrfs snapshot schedule, Seafile history/retention days. Confirm, don't assume.
> - If you add a round-trip canary, write the heartbeat into an at-risk production tree (or rotate through several), not a dedicated library that would stay inside the watched set and sail through while real libraries rot. The probe's load is negligible; its blind spot is the concern.

---

Want me to go ahead and (a) measure the exact worktree directory count to size the limit, and (b) raise `max_user_watches` + add the `seafile-ignore.txt` for `@eaDir`? The sysctl change and ignore file are the actual fix — everything else is monitoring on top. I'd need to use the NAS's write capabilities (preview-first), so I'll show you the preview before anything executes.

content.text:
This is the decisive constraint I have to surface before anything else: **I checked every write capability the NAS MCP exposes, and none of them can do what task 3b needs.** There are specific sysctl setters (`set_vm_overcommit_memory`, `set_vm_dirty_ratios`, `set_io_scheduler`, `set_ionice`) but **none for inotify**; there is **no arbitrary file-write** capability (so no `seafile-ignore.txt`); and `write_tasks` can only run/enable/disable *existing* DSM tasks, not create a boot task. `run_command` blocks all writes at the validator.

Your own deployment notes mention a documented base64 bypass for the `docker` string-block — that could likely apply the changes, but it deliberately circumvents the read-only guard, so I won't fire writes through it without your explicit go-ahead. More on that below.

Let me deliver the parts I can do now — the writeups and the analysis. Writing the root-cause record:

input.content:
---
name: Seafile sync false-synchronized — root cause (inotify watch exhaustion)
description: Why seaf-cli on edgesynology1 silently reports "synchronized" while diverging — inotify max_user_watches=8192 starves the worktree monitor
metadata:
  type: project
---
On 2026-06-19, Generic Decor (under the `decor` library) drifted: seaf-cli reported **synchronized** while the NAS worktree and the server head disagreed. A restart "fixed" it, but that only masked the real bug. Related: [[Seafile Pro Deployment — designflow.app]].

## Root cause — the NAS watcher went blind (NOT a stale/corrupt index)
The `seaf-cli` client runs in the `seaf-cli` Docker container on edgesynology1 (`seaf-daemon`, config `-c /root/.ccnet`, worktree libraries mounted at `/library/{art,char,decor,guides}`). Its log `/root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log` contains, **11,077 times**:
`wt-monitor-linux.c(542): [wt mon] fail to add watch to /library/decor: No space left on device`

"No space left on device" here is **NOT disk** (disk 52% used, 53 TB free; clocks NAS↔server within ~20s). It is the **Linux inotify watch limit**: `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` (the low default). This is a per-UID pool shared by ALL root-owned processes on the host (DSM services + every container). seaf-daemon could only register **3,209 watches** before the shared pool was exhausted, then every further directory — including `/library/decor`, which contains Generic Decor — got **no watch**.

**Mechanism:** seaf-daemon only re-indexes/commits a library when its inotify monitor fires a change event for it. An unwatched directory never fires → edits there are never detected → the daemon believes nothing changed → `seaf-cli status` honestly reports "synchronized" while the worktree has diverged. That is the false-synchronized state.

**Why the restart only masked it:** startup runs a one-time full re-index that caught the drift once, but the watch errors are timestamped *during that same restart* — the daemon is blind again immediately. It WILL recur on any library past the watch ceiling. A watchdog that "detect drift → restart daemon" would hide this permanently (each restart's full scan resets the symptom; root cause persists).

## Measurements (2026-06-19)
Worktree directory counts (`find -type d`):
- art 83,851 (80,131 inside `@eaDir`) · char 377,816 (319,996) · decor 28,103 (22,220) · guides 51,321 (22,352)
- **Total ≈ 541,000 dirs; ~444,700 (82%) are Synology `@eaDir` thumbnail folders.** Real content dirs ≈ 96,400.
- seaf-daemon held 3,209 watches; host RAM 32 GB.

## Fix
1. **Raise `fs.inotify.max_user_watches` to 1,048,576** (covers full 541k worktree + growth even if ignore doesn't reduce watches). `max_user_instances` → 1024. ~1 KiB pinned kernel mem per watch used (≈96 MB at real load; ≤1 GB worst case of 32 GB). 400k is too low — below the current 541k dir count, so it would NOT fix the bug unless @eaDir is also excluded.
2. **Persist across reboot.** On this host `/etc/sysctl.conf` IS honored (the nas-api `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` capability writes there). Add the inotify line there, or a DSM boot-up Task Scheduler task running `sysctl -w`.
3. **Exclude junk** via `seafile-ignore.txt` in each library root (`@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `.DS_Store`) — cuts dirs ~82%, reduces server junk + scan time. Note: adding ignore does NOT delete already-synced server copies; uncertain whether Seafile's wt-monitor skips watches for ignored dirs, so do NOT rely on it for the watch fix — the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.
4. **Restart seaf-daemon**, then verify: `grep -c 'No space left on device'` for entries after the restart must be **0**.

## Tooling constraint
nas-direct MCP has NO capability to set inotify sysctl, write arbitrary files, or create boot tasks (`write_tasks` only runs/enables/disables existing tasks). `run_command` blocks writes. Apply via the documented base64 run_command bypass (see CONTEXT_FOR_AI.md), via DSM directly, or by adding a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api. edgesynology1 is the source of truth (NAS always wins), so conflict direction is fixed.

content.text: Now the implementation plan covering the fix plus all 7 "what else is missing" points:

input.content:
# Seafile NAS Sync — Implementation Plan

**Context:** edgesynology1 (`seaf-cli` container) is the authoritative source of truth, syncing
`/library/{art,char,decor,guides}` to seafile.designflow.app. On 2026-06-19 Generic Decor drifted while
`seaf-cli status` reported "synchronized."

**Root cause (confirmed):** `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` (default). The worktree has ~541,000
directories (82% are `@eaDir` thumbnail junk). The shared root inotify pool is exhausted at ~3,209
watches, so the worktree monitor goes blind on most directories. Unwatched dirs never raise change
events, so the daemon never re-indexes them and reports "synchronized" while diverging. A restart only
masks it via a one-time full scan.

---

## Phase 0 — Safety net (do first)
- [ ] `create_prechange_snapshot` on /volume1 (Btrfs recovery point). [nas-api capability — available]
- [ ] Confirm recovery nets are ON (see Phase 4 #7) before touching sync.

## Phase 1 — Fix the root cause (the actual repair)
- [ ] **Raise the watch limit (live):** `sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_watches=1048576 fs.inotify.max_user_instances=1024`
      - 1,048,576 chosen to cover the full 541k worktree + growth even if ignore doesn't reduce watches.
      - Cost: ~1 KiB pinned kernel RAM per watch actually held (~96 MB real, ≤1 GB worst case of 32 GB).
      - 400k is too low — below the current 541k dir count.
- [ ] **Persist across reboot:** append to `/etc/sysctl.conf` (honored on this host — nas-api's
      `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` already writes there), AND/OR a DSM Task Scheduler boot-up task
      (root) running the `sysctl -w`. Verify it survives a reboot.
- [ ] **Reduce junk (optional but high-value):** add `seafile-ignore.txt` to each library root with
      `@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `.DS_Store`. Cuts directory/scan load ~82% and stops syncing
      thumbnails to the server. Note: does NOT delete already-synced server copies; clean those
      separately if desired. Do not rely on this for the watch fix.
- [ ] **Restart seaf-daemon** so it can register watches across the whole worktree.
- [ ] **Verify:** after restart, `grep -c 'No space left on device' /root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log`
      (entries after restart) must be **0**. Spot-check that `/library/decor` now has a watch.

**Tooling note:** nas-direct MCP has no inotify-sysctl / file-write / boot-task-create capability and
`run_command` blocks writes. Apply via (a) the documented base64 run_command bypass, (b) DSM directly,
or (c) add a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api (preferred long-term — see Phase 3).

---

## Phase 2 — Watchdog (defense-in-depth), addressing the 7 gaps

**#1 Root cause was unknown → now fixed.** Watchdog must monitor the *cause*, not just the symptom:
- [ ] Alert if `fail to add watch` / `No space left on device` appears in seafile.log (must stay 0).
- [ ] Track inotify headroom: `watches_in_use / max_user_watches`; warn at >80%.

**#2 Use Seafile's own truth, not filesystem signatures:**
- [ ] Per library, compare the client's synced-to commit ID vs the server's head commit ID. Exact and
      cheap. Flag "client claims server head but live folder differs" as the false-synchronized signal.
- [ ] (If a content check is ever needed, walk recursively — never root-only — but commit IDs make it
      unnecessary.)

**#3 Canary that actually catches this failure:**
- [ ] Don't use a cozy dedicated heartbeat library (it would stay inside the watched set and pass while
      real libraries rot). Either rely on the log + commit-ID signals above, or write a rotating
      heartbeat file *into at-risk production trees* and confirm round-trip within an SLA.
- [ ] Probe load is negligible (few-KB write + metadata read); effectiveness is the concern, not cost.

**#4 Alerting reaches a human:**
- [ ] On quarantine, push an actual alert (email/notification), not just a dashboard status.

**#5 Anti-flap:**
- [ ] Never auto-restart as the remedy (restart is what hid this). On anomaly: capture diagnostics
      (watch-error count, inotify usage, client-vs-server head, disk, daemon uptime), set a clear RED
      status, alert a human. If any auto-action is added later, use backoff + a max-restart cap, then
      stay quarantined.

**#6 Cover everything:**
- [ ] Per-library state machine across ALL libraries (art, char, decor, guides) on BOTH edgesynology1
      and edgesynology2. edgesynology1 is authoritative, so resolution direction is fixed (NAS wins) —
      the watchdog only verifies NAS edits reach the server.

**#7 Verify recovery nets are actually ON (don't assume):**
- [ ] Synology recycle bin enabled per share.
- [ ] Btrfs snapshot schedule active (Snapshot Replication) with sane retention.
- [ ] Seafile server history/retention days configured for these libraries.

---

## Phase 3 — Hardening / long-term
- [ ] Add a `set_inotify_watches` (live + persist) capability to nas-api so this is a sanctioned,
      previewable operation instead of a base64 bypass.
- [ ] Consider deprioritizing seaf-daemon I/O during big scans (`set_ionice`) if it crowds foreground.
- [ ] Clean already-synced `@eaDir` from the server to reclaim space (separate, careful pass).

## Success criteria
- 0 `No space left on device` watch errors after a daemon restart and after a reboot.
- Edit a file in each library on the NAS → appears on the server within the expected window, with no
  manual restart.
- Watchdog reports per-library GREEN only after commit-ID match AND zero watch errors; RED + human
  alert otherwise.

toolUseResult.content:
# Seafile NAS Sync — Implementation Plan

**Context:** edgesynology1 (`seaf-cli` container) is the authoritative source of truth, syncing
`/library/{art,char,decor,guides}` to seafile.designflow.app. On 2026-06-19 Generic Decor drifted while
`seaf-cli status` reported "synchronized."

**Root cause (confirmed):** `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` (default). The worktree has ~541,000
directories (82% are `@eaDir` thumbnail junk). The shared root inotify pool is exhausted at ~3,209
watches, so the worktree monitor goes blind on most directories. Unwatched dirs never raise change
events, so the daemon never re-indexes them and reports "synchronized" while diverging. A restart only
masks it via a one-time full scan.

---

## Phase 0 — Safety net (do first)
- [ ] `create_prechange_snapshot` on /volume1 (Btrfs recovery point). [nas-api capability — available]
- [ ] Confirm recovery nets are ON (see Phase 4 #7) before touching sync.

## Phase 1 — Fix the root cause (the actual repair)
- [ ] **Raise the watch limit (live):** `sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_watches=1048576 fs.inotify.max_user_instances=1024`
      - 1,048,576 chosen to cover the full 541k worktree + growth even if ignore doesn't reduce watches.
      - Cost: ~1 KiB pinned kernel RAM per watch actually held (~96 MB real, ≤1 GB worst case of 32 GB).
      - 400k is too low — below the current 541k dir count.
- [ ] **Persist across reboot:** append to `/etc/sysctl.conf` (honored on this host — nas-api's
      `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` already writes there), AND/OR a DSM Task Scheduler boot-up task
      (root) running the `sysctl -w`. Verify it survives a reboot.
- [ ] **Reduce junk (optional but high-value):** add `seafile-ignore.txt` to each library root with
      `@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `.DS_Store`. Cuts directory/scan load ~82% and stops syncing
      thumbnails to the server. Note: does NOT delete already-synced server copies; clean those
      separately if desired. Do not rely on this for the watch fix.
- [ ] **Restart seaf-daemon** so it can register watches across the whole worktree.
- [ ] **Verify:** after restart, `grep -c 'No space left on device' /root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log`
      (entries after restart) must be **0**. Spot-check that `/library/decor` now has a watch.

**Tooling note:** nas-direct MCP has no inotify-sysctl / file-write / boot-task-create capability and
`run_command` blocks writes. Apply via (a) the documented base64 run_command bypass, (b) DSM directly,
or (c) add a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api (preferred long-term — see Phase 3).

---

## Phase 2 — Watchdog (defense-in-depth), addressing the 7 gaps

**#1 Root cause was unknown → now fixed.** Watchdog must monitor the *cause*, not just the symptom:
- [ ] Alert if `fail to add watch` / `No space left on device` appears in seafile.log (must stay 0).
- [ ] Track inotify headroom: `watches_in_use / max_user_watches`; warn at >80%.

**#2 Use Seafile's own truth, not filesystem signatures:**
- [ ] Per library, compare the client's synced-to commit ID vs the server's head commit ID. Exact and
      cheap. Flag "client claims server head but live folder differs" as the false-synchronized signal.
- [ ] (If a content check is ever needed, walk recursively — never root-only — but commit IDs make it
      unnecessary.)

**#3 Canary that actually catches this failure:**
- [ ] Don't use a cozy dedicated heartbeat library (it would stay inside the watched set and pass while
      real libraries rot). Either rely on the log + commit-ID signals above, or write a rotating
      heartbeat file *into at-risk production trees* and confirm round-trip within an SLA.
- [ ] Probe load is negligible (few-KB write + metadata read); effectiveness is the concern, not cost.

**#4 Alerting reaches a human:**
- [ ] On quarantine, push an actual alert (email/notification), not just a dashboard status.

**#5 Anti-flap:**
- [ ] Never auto-restart as the remedy (restart is what hid this). On anomaly: capture diagnostics
      (watch-error count, inotify usage, client-vs-server head, disk, daemon uptime), set a clear RED
      status, alert a human. If any auto-action is added later, use backoff + a max-restart cap, then
      stay quarantined.

**#6 Cover everything:**
- [ ] Per-library state machine across ALL libraries (art, char, decor, guides) on BOTH edgesynology1
      and edgesynology2. edgesynology1 is authoritative, so resolution direction is fixed (NAS wins) —
      the watchdog only verifies NAS edits reach the server.

**#7 Verify recovery nets are actually ON (don't assume):**
- [ ] Synology recycle bin enabled per share.
- [ ] Btrfs snapshot schedule active (Snapshot Replication) with sane retention.
- [ ] Seafile server history/retention days configured for these libraries.

---

## Phase 3 — Hardening / long-term
- [ ] Add a `set_inotify_watches` (live + persist) capability to nas-api so this is a sanctioned,
      previewable operation instead of a base64 bypass.
- [ ] Consider deprioritizing seaf-daemon I/O during big scans (`set_ionice`) if it crowds foreground.
- [ ] Clean already-synced `@eaDir` from the server to reclaim space (separate, careful pass).

## Success criteria
- 0 `No space left on device` watch errors after a daemon restart and after a reboot.
- Edit a file in each library on the NAS → appears on the server within the expected window, with no
  manual restart.
- Watchdog reports per-library GREEN only after commit-ID match AND zero watch errors; RED + human
  alert otherwise.

All four tasks are done as far as I can take them without write access. Summary:

## 1) Root cause — written to the `.md` files ✅
Created `project_seafile_sync_rootcause.md` in your memory dir (detailed: mechanism, evidence, measurements, fix, tooling constraint), indexed it in `MEMORY.md`, and cross-linked it to the deployment memory.

## 2) Downside of `max_user_watches = 400,000`
Two separate things — and the real problem isn't the one people usually worry about:

- **Memory (the usual worry): negligible.** Each watch pins ~1 KiB of non-swappable kernel RAM, allocated only as watches are actually held. 400k fully used ≈ ~400 MB; 1M ≈ ~1 GB — out of 32 GB, i.e. ~1–3%. The difference between 400k and 1M is trivial here.
- **The actual problem: 400k is below your current directory count.** The worktree is **~541,000 dirs today and growing.** At 400k, the daemon would still exhaust watches and stay blind on ~140k+ dirs — **the bug would persist.** 400k only works *if* you also exclude `@eaDir` (which drops the real need to ~96k) — and even then it leaves little headroom.
- **Recommendation: set `1,048,576`.** It covers the full worktree with ~10× headroom even if the ignore file turns out not to reduce watches (Seafile may watch ignored dirs anyway — unverified), so the fix doesn't depend on anything uncertain. Memory cost is a rounding error.

## 3a) Directory measurement ✅
~541,000 dirs total; **82% (~444,700) are `@eaDir` thumbnail junk**; ~96,400 real content dirs. (Per-library table is in the root-cause memory.)

## 3b) Raising the limit + writing `seafile-ignore.txt` — blocked on tooling/permission
I **cannot** execute these with the sanctioned MCP surface — confirmed by enumerating every write group:
- No inotify sysctl setter (only `vm.overcommit`, `dirty_ratios`, `io_scheduler`, `ionice` exist).
- No arbitrary file-write capability → can't create `seafile-ignore.txt`.
- `write_tasks` can't *create* a boot task, only run/enable/disable existing ones.
- `run_command` blocks all writes.

The only in-session path is the **base64 bypass** your deployment notes reference — but that deliberately defeats the read-only guard, so I want your explicit OK before I push writes through it. Also flagging: the `seafile-ignore.txt` is genuinely useful but should be treated as secondary (it won't reliably fix watches and needs care so it doesn't churn already-synced server thumbnails) — **the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.**

Wrote `/home/ai/seafile-sync-fix-plan.md` — phased (safety snapshot → root-cause fix → watchdog → hardening), with the watchdog section explicitly addressing all 7 "what else is missing" points, concrete commands, and success criteria.

content.text:
Verified across every library root (art/char/decor/guides), the `/library` root, the worktree, and the host path: **there are no ignore files anywhere — not `seafile-ignore.txt`, not `.seafile-ignore`.**

So, directly answering "is it accurate?":

**The reasoning is mostly right, but the factual claim is wrong, and there's a filename bug.**

1. **"what we now have in the worktree .seafile-ignore files" — false.** Those files don't exist on disk by either name. (Consistent with what we just established: that session had no write capability and `run_command` blocks writes, so it likely couldn't actually create them — only believed it had.)

2. **Wrong filename.** Seafile's ignore file must be named **exactly `seafile-ignore.txt`** and sit at each library's root. **`.seafile-ignore` is not recognized** — even if it existed, it would do nothing.

3. **The two concepts they stated are accurate:**
   - Ignoring prevents syncing *new/changed* `@eaDir` going forward. ✓
   - `@eaDir` already on the server won't be auto-removed by adding the ignore; it must be deleted from the library separately once confirmed junk. ✓

4. **Add these caveats they missed:**
   - Synology **continuously regenerates** `@eaDir` thumbnails on the NAS, so they'll keep reappearing locally — the ignore only stops *re-uploading* them. That's expected, not a failure.
   - The ignore file does **not** reliably reduce inotify watches (the monitor may watch ignored dirs anyway), so it is **not** the fix for the drift — the `max_user_watches` raise is. Treat the ignore file as cleanup/hygiene, not the repair.
   - The patterns themselves (`@eaDir #recycle #snapshot @tmp .DS_Store Thumbs.db *.tmp`) are good and correctly use Synology's `#recycle`/`#snapshot` names.

**The unifying point given your "add nas-api capability first" choice:** writing those ignore files needs the *same* missing write capability as the sysctl change. So one capability-add unblocks both. I'd add to nas-api:
- `set_inotify_watches` (live `sysctl -w` + persist to `/etc/sysctl.conf`), and
- a previewable file-write helper (or a purpose-built `write_seafile_ignore` that drops `seafile-ignore.txt` into each library root).

Then the whole Phase 1 runs through sanctioned, preview-required tools instead of the base64 bypass.

---
name: seafile-sync-false-synchronized-root-cause-inotify-watch-exhaustion
description: "Why seaf-cli on edgesynology1 silently reports \"synchronized\" while diverging — inotify max_user_watches=8192 starves the worktree monitor"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 8e5209fb-6aac-4cff-aba5-9813fd333a2f
---

On 2026-06-19, Generic Decor (under the `decor` library) drifted: seaf-cli reported **synchronized** while the NAS worktree and the server head disagreed. A restart "fixed" it, but that only masked the real bug. Related: [[Seafile Pro Deployment — designflow.app]].

## Root cause — the NAS watcher went blind (NOT a stale/corrupt index)
The `seaf-cli` client runs in the `seaf-cli` Docker container on edgesynology1 (`seaf-daemon`, config `-c /root/.ccnet`, worktree libraries mounted at `/library/{art,char,decor,guides}`). Its log `/root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log` contains, **11,077 times**:
`wt-monitor-linux.c(542): [wt mon] fail to add watch to /library/decor: No space left on device`

"No space left on device" here is **NOT disk** (disk 52% used, 53 TB free; clocks NAS↔server within ~20s). It is the **Linux inotify watch limit**: `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` (the low default). This is a per-UID pool shared by ALL root-owned processes on the host (DSM services + every container). seaf-daemon could only register **3,209 watches** before the shared pool was exhausted, then every further directory — including `/library/decor`, which contains Generic Decor — got **no watch**.

**Mechanism:** seaf-daemon only re-indexes/commits a library when its inotify monitor fires a change event for it. An unwatched directory never fires → edits there are never detected → the daemon believes nothing changed → `seaf-cli status` honestly reports "synchronized" while the worktree has diverged. That is the false-synchronized state.

**Why the restart only masked it:** startup runs a one-time full re-index that caught the drift once, but the watch errors are timestamped *during that same restart* — the daemon is blind again immediately. It WILL recur on any library past the watch ceiling. A watchdog that "detect drift → restart daemon" would hide this permanently (each restart's full scan resets the symptom; root cause persists).

## Measurements (2026-06-19)
Worktree directory counts (`find -type d`):
- art 83,851 (80,131 inside `@eaDir`) · char 377,816 (319,996) · decor 28,103 (22,220) · guides 51,321 (22,352)
- **Total ≈ 541,000 dirs; ~444,700 (82%) are Synology `@eaDir` thumbnail folders.** Real content dirs ≈ 96,400.
- seaf-daemon held 3,209 watches; host RAM 32 GB.

## Fix
1. **Raise `fs.inotify.max_user_watches` to 1,048,576** (covers full 541k worktree + growth even if ignore doesn't reduce watches). `max_user_instances` → 1024. ~1 KiB pinned kernel mem per watch used (≈96 MB at real load; ≤1 GB worst case of 32 GB). 400k is too low — below the current 541k dir count, so it would NOT fix the bug unless @eaDir is also excluded.
2. **Persist across reboot.** On this host `/etc/sysctl.conf` IS honored (the nas-api `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` capability writes there). Add the inotify line there, or a DSM boot-up Task Scheduler task running `sysctl -w`.
3. **Exclude junk** via `seafile-ignore.txt` in each library root (`@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `.DS_Store`) — cuts dirs ~82%, reduces server junk + scan time. Note: adding ignore does NOT delete already-synced server copies; uncertain whether Seafile's wt-monitor skips watches for ignored dirs, so do NOT rely on it for the watch fix — the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.
4. **Restart seaf-daemon**, then verify: `grep -c 'No space left on device'` for entries after the restart must be **0**.

## Tooling constraint
nas-direct MCP has NO capability to set inotify sysctl, write arbitrary files, or create boot tasks (`write_tasks` only runs/enables/disables existing tasks). `run_command` blocks writes. Apply via the documented base64 run_command bypass (see CONTEXT_FOR_AI.md), via DSM directly, or by adding a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api. edgesynology1 is the source of truth (NAS always wins), so conflict direction is fixed.

2. **Persist across reboot.** On this host `/etc/sysctl.conf` IS honored (the nas-api `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` capability writes there). Add the inotify line there, or a DSM boot-up Task Scheduler task running `sysctl -w`.

-3. **Exclude junk** via `seafile-ignore.txt` in each library root (`@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `.DS_Store`) — cuts dirs ~82%, reduces server junk + scan time. Note: adding ignore does NOT delete already-synced server copies; uncertain whether Seafile's wt-monitor skips watches for ignored dirs, so do NOT rely on it for the watch fix — the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.

+3. **Exclude junk** via `seafile-ignore.txt` in each library root (`@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `@tmp`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `*.tmp`) — cuts dirs ~82%, reduces server junk + scan time. **Verified 2026-06-19: NO ignore files exist on disk by any name** (a prior session claimed it created `.seafile-ignore` files but none are present — and the name is wrong anyway). The file MUST be named exactly `seafile-ignore.txt` at each library root; `.seafile-ignore` is not recognized by Seafile. Caveats: adding ignore does NOT delete already-synced server copies (remove separately); Synology constantly regenerates `@eaDir` locally so they reappear (ignore only stops re-upload); uncertain whether the wt-monitor skips watches for ignored dirs, so do NOT rely on it for the watch fix — the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.
4. **Restart seaf-daemon**, then verify: `grep -c 'No space left on device'` for entries after the restart must be **0**.

2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	These rules exist so AI tools can safely assist with this repo without creating production drift.
6	
7	## System of truth
8	
9	- GitHub is the source of truth for code, Docker Compose, Dockerfiles, and workflows.
10	- Coolify is the source of truth for production runtime environment variables and deployment target settings.
11	- The production server is only a runtime host, not a configuration source.
12	
13	## Branch policy
14	
15	- This repo uses one branch only: `main`
16	- Do not propose or create feature branches
17	- Do not suggest branch-based workflows
18	- Do not assume there is a staging branch
19	- All approved changes should target `main`
20	
21	## Approved deployment path
22	
23	The only normal deployment path is:
24	
25	1. change files in this repo
26	2. commit to `main`
27	3. GitHub Actions builds and pushes the image to GHCR
28	
29	For `apps/web` and `apps/nas-mcp`:
30	
31	4. GitHub Actions calls the Coolify webhook
32	5. Coolify redeploys automatically
33	
34	For `apps/agent` and `apps/nas-api`:
35	
36	4. Watchtower on each NAS detects the new image and automatically recreates the containers (every 5 min poll)
37	
38	Do not propose alternate routine deployment methods.
39	
40	Note: Watchtower updates the **image** only, not the compose config. Changes to
41	`deploy/synology/docker-compose.agent.yml` (new mounts, capabilities, or env keys)
42	require a one-time `docker compose up -d` on each NAS to apply — this is the
43	documented exception, not a routine deploy path. See `docs/deployment.md`.
44	
45	## Allowed AI actions
46	
47	AI may help with:
48	
49	- editing application code
50	- editing `docker-compose.yml`
51	- editing Dockerfiles
52	- editing GitHub Actions workflows
53	- editing documentation
54	- recommending GitHub Secrets usage for CI/CD
55	- applying Coolify runtime environment variable changes directly through the Coolify API or UI (env vars, feature flags, health checks, restart policy, domain bindings — settings Coolify owns)
56	- triggering deployment through the approved GitHub -> Coolify path
57	
58	## Forbidden AI actions
59	
60	AI must not:
61	
62	- use SSH as the normal deployment path
63	- hand-edit files directly on the production server
64	- assume the server contains the source of truth
65	- create undocumented hotfixes on the live machine
66	- introduce additional branches
67	- create a second deployment system
68	- recommend storing production runtime configuration only in ad hoc server files
69	
70	## NAS maintenance safety
71	
72	Direct SSH maintenance on the production NASes is exceptional and must be
73	operator-requested. For any file-tree audit, snapshot comparison, timestamp
74	repair, or other metadata-heavy NAS command, use the installed Entware `ionice`
75	wrapper and schedule full runs for quiet windows:
76	
77	```sh
78	/opt/bin/ionice -c3 nice -n 19 <command>
79	```
80	
81	Both `edgesynology1` and `edgesynology2` have `/opt/bin/ionice` installed as of
82	2026-06-15. The wrapper lowers impact, but does not make large metadata crawls
83	free; avoid running them while SMB users are active.
84	
85	For timestamp repair work, treat `edgesynology2` as an evidence source by
86	default. Apply repairs only to `edgesynology1` unless the operator explicitly
87	requests writes on both NASes. Writing the same metadata to both sides can cause
88	ShareSync to replace file entries and churn inodes.
89	
90	## Secrets rule
91	
92	- GitHub Secrets are for CI/CD and build-time secrets
93	- Coolify stores production runtime environment variables
94	- Do not move all runtime secrets into GitHub if the running app is managed by Coolify
95	
96	## Compose rule
97	
98	- The repo copy of `docker-compose.yml` is authoritative
99	- If a service exists, it should be declared in the repo
100	- Do not assume server-side Compose changes are valid unless they are committed
101	
102	## Change discipline
103	
104	When making changes:
105	
106	- prefer small, explicit edits
107	- preserve the single-branch workflow
108	- keep deployment logic simple
109	- avoid introducing tools or processes that require manual server babysitting
110	
111	## Decision preference
112	
113	When multiple valid options exist, prefer the option that:
114	
115	- keeps `main` as the single source of truth
116	- keeps production behavior reproducible
117	- reduces hidden state on the server
118	- is easier for a non-developer owner to understand and audit

file.content:
# AI Operating Rules

## Purpose

These rules exist so AI tools can safely assist with this repo without creating production drift.

## System of truth

- GitHub is the source of truth for code, Docker Compose, Dockerfiles, and workflows.
- Coolify is the source of truth for production runtime environment variables and deployment target settings.
- The production server is only a runtime host, not a configuration source.

## Branch policy

- This repo uses one branch only: `main`
- Do not propose or create feature branches
- Do not suggest branch-based workflows
- Do not assume there is a staging branch
- All approved changes should target `main`

## Approved deployment path

The only normal deployment path is:

1. change files in this repo
2. commit to `main`
3. GitHub Actions builds and pushes the image to GHCR

For `apps/web` and `apps/nas-mcp`:

4. GitHub Actions calls the Coolify webhook
5. Coolify redeploys automatically

For `apps/agent` and `apps/nas-api`:

4. Watchtower on each NAS detects the new image and automatically recreates the containers (every 5 min poll)

Do not propose alternate routine deployment methods.

Note: Watchtower updates the **image** only, not the compose config. Changes to
`deploy/synology/docker-compose.agent.yml` (new mounts, capabilities, or env keys)
require a one-time `docker compose up -d` on each NAS to apply — this is the
documented exception, not a routine deploy path. See `docs/deployment.md`.

## Allowed AI actions

AI may help with:

- editing application code
- editing `docker-compose.yml`
- editing Dockerfiles
- editing GitHub Actions workflows
- editing documentation
- recommending GitHub Secrets usage for CI/CD
- applying Coolify runtime environment variable changes directly through the Coolify API or UI (env vars, feature flags, health checks, restart policy, domain bindings — settings Coolify owns)
- triggering deployment through the approved GitHub -> Coolify path

## Forbidden AI actions

AI must not:

- use SSH as the normal deployment path
- hand-edit files directly on the production server
- assume the server contains the source of truth
- create undocumented hotfixes on the live machine
- introduce additional branches
- create a second deployment system
- recommend storing production runtime configuration only in ad hoc server files

## NAS maintenance safety

Direct SSH maintenance on the production NASes is exceptional and must be
operator-requested. For any file-tree audit, snapshot comparison, timestamp
repair, or other metadata-heavy NAS command, use the installed Entware `ionice`
wrapper and schedule full runs for quiet windows:

```sh
/opt/bin/ionice -c3 nice -n 19 <command>
```

Both `edgesynology1` and `edgesynology2` have `/opt/bin/ionice` installed as of
2026-06-15. The wrapper lowers impact, but does not make large metadata crawls
free; avoid running them while SMB users are active.

For timestamp repair work, treat `edgesynology2` as an evidence source by
default. Apply repairs only to `edgesynology1` unless the operator explicitly
requests writes on both NASes. Writing the same metadata to both sides can cause
ShareSync to replace file entries and churn inodes.

## Secrets rule

- GitHub Secrets are for CI/CD and build-time secrets
- Coolify stores production runtime environment variables
- Do not move all runtime secrets into GitHub if the running app is managed by Coolify

## Compose rule

- The repo copy of `docker-compose.yml` is authoritative
- If a service exists, it should be declared in the repo
- Do not assume server-side Compose changes are valid unless they are committed

## Change discipline

When making changes:

- prefer small, explicit edits
- preserve the single-branch workflow
- keep deployment logic simple
- avoid introducing tools or processes that require manual server babysitting

## Decision preference

When multiple valid options exist, prefer the option that:

- keeps `main` as the single source of truth
- keeps production behavior reproducible
- reduces hidden state on the server
- is easier for a non-developer owner to understand and audit

1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	**Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) first.** It is the canonical operating guide for this repo and applies to all AI sessions. The notes below are Claude Code-specific only.
4	
5	## Allowed operations
6	
7	- Edit source under `apps/`, `docs/`, `deploy/`, `supabase/migrations/`, `.github/workflows/`, top-level `*.md`.
8	- Run `pnpm build`, `pnpm lint`, `pnpm type-check`, `go build`, `go test` locally.
9	- Commit and push to `main` when explicitly asked. Default behavior: do not commit unprompted.
10	
11	## Not allowed
12	
13	- Direct SSH to the VPS or NAS is **not** a normal deployment path. The VPS has public SSH disabled by design. Do not propose SSH-based deploys, manual `docker build` on the VPS, or runtime container manipulation.
14	- Runtime env changes belong in Coolify — apply them directly through the Coolify API or UI. Do not route them through GitHub Actions shell commands or SSH. See `AI_OPERATING_RULES.md`.
15	- Do not create feature branches. This repo uses one branch: `main`.
16	- Do not "fix" the items listed under [AGENTS.md § 10 — Intentional quirks](AGENTS.md) without reading the linked incident/commit first.
17	
18	## Commit style
19	
20	- Subject line: `area: short imperative` (e.g. `nas-mcp: …`, `agent: …`, `docs: …`). Match prior history (`git log --oneline`) for the project area.
21	- Body: one short paragraph on *why*, not what. Reference incident, prior commit, or user request when relevant.
22	- Co-author trailer: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>` (match the model in use).
23	
24	## Ignore files
25	
26	`.claudeignore` at the repo root is honored by Claude Code. It excludes build artifacts (`node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/`, `.turbo/`), lockfiles, `evals/`, and the vestigial scratch file `ersahazan2Desktopsynology-monitor`. Update it when adding new generated directories.
27	
28	For other AI tools, paste `AGENTS.md` as your first message and follow the *§ 9 — What to ignore* list.
29	
30	## Memory / context notes
31	
32	- Local workspace path: `/worksp/monitor/app`. Working copy of `github.com/u2giants/synology-monitor` on `main`.
33	- The NAS MCP server has 119 shared tools in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but exposes only 7 small tools per session (`list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`). `check_disk_space` and `restart_nas_api` are registry tools registered eagerly by `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`. When debugging tool availability, check catalog/search/detail results, `tools-config.json` enablement, and `EAGER_TOOLS`.
34	- The 3-stage AI pipeline (`stage1-structurer.ts`, `stage2-reasoning.ts`, `stage3-explainer.ts`) is the only active issue-agent pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and OpenRouter inference path have been removed.
35	- `issue_evidence_items` and `issue_evidence` are different tables with different purposes — do not confuse them.
36	- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not yet wired to any pipeline stage.
37	- `drive_team_folders_partitioned`: schema exists, no child partitions, no writes — future scaling infrastructure; do not drop.
38	- HANDOFF.md is created only when work is genuinely incomplete at session end. Delete it as part of the commit that resolves the work it describes.
39	- nas-api has a native **archive job system** (`apps/nas-api/internal/jobs/`) behind `/jobs/*` REST endpoints, persisting to a durable `/app/data/jobs` bind mount (design in `docs/synology-archive.md`, build guide in `docs/synology-archive-implementation.md`). **Phase 1** = read-only file inventory (`/jobs/inventory/*`). **Phase 2** = staged, reversible archive move (`/jobs/archive-move/*`: plan→preflight→snapshot→execute→verify→rollback, plus `clean_empty_dirs`); execute/rollback are tier-3 and write via the **writable `/btrfs/volume1/<share>`** mount (not the `:ro` `/volume1/<share>`). Both use `NAS_API_NAME` (the logical `edgesynology1/2` name), **deliberately separate** from the agent's `NAS_NAME`. The jobs mount/env need a one-time `docker compose up -d` per NAS; until then the endpoints return 503. Btrfs snapshot/subvol ops are behind an injectable interface (`fsOps`) so the move logic is unit-tested on temp trees; the real btrfs path is validated live.
40

**Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) first.** It is the canonical operating guide for this repo and applies to all AI sessions. The notes below are Claude Code-specific only.

## Allowed operations

- Edit source under `apps/`, `docs/`, `deploy/`, `supabase/migrations/`, `.github/workflows/`, top-level `*.md`.
- Run `pnpm build`, `pnpm lint`, `pnpm type-check`, `go build`, `go test` locally.
- Commit and push to `main` when explicitly asked. Default behavior: do not commit unprompted.

## Not allowed

- Direct SSH to the VPS or NAS is **not** a normal deployment path. The VPS has public SSH disabled by design. Do not propose SSH-based deploys, manual `docker build` on the VPS, or runtime container manipulation.
- Runtime env changes belong in Coolify — apply them directly through the Coolify API or UI. Do not route them through GitHub Actions shell commands or SSH. See `AI_OPERATING_RULES.md`.
- Do not create feature branches. This repo uses one branch: `main`.
- Do not "fix" the items listed under [AGENTS.md § 10 — Intentional quirks](AGENTS.md) without reading the linked incident/commit first.

## Commit style

- Subject line: `area: short imperative` (e.g. `nas-mcp: …`, `agent: …`, `docs: …`). Match prior history (`git log --oneline`) for the project area.
- Body: one short paragraph on *why*, not what. Reference incident, prior commit, or user request when relevant.
- Co-author trailer: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>` (match the model in use).

## Ignore files

`.claudeignore` at the repo root is honored by Claude Code. It excludes build artifacts (`node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/`, `.turbo/`), lockfiles, `evals/`, and the vestigial scratch file `ersahazan2Desktopsynology-monitor`. Update it when adding new generated directories.

For other AI tools, paste `AGENTS.md` as your first message and follow the *§ 9 — What to ignore* list.

## Memory / context notes

- Local workspace path: `/worksp/monitor/app`. Working copy of `github.com/u2giants/synology-monitor` on `main`.
- The NAS MCP server has 119 shared tools in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but exposes only 7 small tools per session (`list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`). `check_disk_space` and `restart_nas_api` are registry tools registered eagerly by `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`. When debugging tool availability, check catalog/search/detail results, `tools-config.json` enablement, and `EAGER_TOOLS`.
- The 3-stage AI pipeline (`stage1-structurer.ts`, `stage2-reasoning.ts`, `stage3-explainer.ts`) is the only active issue-agent pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and OpenRouter inference path have been removed.
- `issue_evidence_items` and `issue_evidence` are different tables with different purposes — do not confuse them.
- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not yet wired to any pipeline stage.
- `drive_team_folders_partitioned`: schema exists, no child partitions, no writes — future scaling infrastructure; do not drop.
- HANDOFF.md is created only when work is genuinely incomplete at session end. Delete it as part of the commit that resolves the work it describes.
- nas-api has a native **archive job system** (`apps/nas-api/internal/jobs/`) behind `/jobs/*` REST endpoints, persisting to a durable `/app/data/jobs` bind mount (design in `docs/synology-archive.md`, build guide in `docs/synology-archive-implementation.md`). **Phase 1** = read-only file inventory (`/jobs/inventory/*`). **Phase 2** = staged, reversible archive move (`/jobs/archive-move/*`: plan→preflight→snapshot→execute→verify→rollback, plus `clean_empty_dirs`); execute/rollback are tier-3 and write via the **writable `/btrfs/volume1/<share>`** mount (not the `:ro` `/volume1/<share>`). Both use `NAS_API_NAME` (the logical `edgesynology1/2` name), **deliberately separate** from the agent's `NAS_NAME`. The jobs mount/env need a one-time `docker compose up -d` per NAS; until then the endpoints return 503. Btrfs snapshot/subvol ops are behind an injectable interface (`fsOps`) so the move logic is unit-tested on temp trees; the real btrfs path is validated live.

file.content:
# Synology Monitor

Telemetry collection, issue detection, and LLM-driven remediation for a two-unit Synology NAS fleet. A Go agent on each NAS pushes metrics and logs to Supabase; a Next.js dashboard groups them into issues and runs a three-stage AI pipeline that diagnoses problems and proposes fixes behind an operator approval gate. An MCP server also exposes 100+ NAS diagnostic tools to AI chat clients.

Live at **[mon.designflow.app](https://mon.designflow.app)**.

## Docs

| | |
|---|---|
| [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) | **Start here** — canonical operating guide for AI sessions and engineers |
| [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) | System design, data flow, component constraints |
| [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) | Build, run, test, debug |
| [docs/configuration.md](docs/configuration.md) | All environment variables |
| [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md) | CI/CD and release workflow |
| [PLAN.md](PLAN.md) | Historical issue-agent rebuild plan; current behavior is documented in `docs/architecture.md` |

## Prerequisites

- **Go 1.23+** with CGO enabled
- **Node.js 22+** and **pnpm 9**
- **Docker**

## Getting started

```sh
pnpm install
pnpm build
```

| Task | Command |
|---|---|
| Run web app locally | `cd apps/web && pnpm dev` (needs `.env.local` — see `docs/development.md`) |
| Build the Go agent | `cd apps/agent && CGO_ENABLED=1 go build ./...` |
| Run type checks | `pnpm type-check` |
| Run Go tests | `cd apps/agent && go test ./...` |

## Where to look for what

| You want to... | Start here |
|---|---|
| Add or modify a NAS diagnostic tool | `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`, `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` |
| Add a new agent telemetry collector | `apps/agent/internal/collector/` + wire in `cmd/agent/main.go` |
| Change how issues are detected or diagnosed | `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-detector.ts`, `ai/pipeline-v2.ts` |
| Allow a new NAS shell command | `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` + `validator_test.go` |

## Deployment

Push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds and pushes images to GHCR. The web app and NAS MCP server redeploy automatically via Coolify webhook; the agent and NAS API are picked up by Watchtower on each NAS within 5 minutes.

That covers ordinary image deploys. Compose changes such as new mounts, capabilities, or env keys require an explicit `docker compose up -d` on the NAS after the file is updated there, because Watchtower does not apply compose-file changes. See [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md) for rollback, pinning, and the exceptional manual-compose path.
