content.content:
1	# Architecture
2	
3	## System purpose
4	
5	Monitors two Synology NAS devices (`edgesynology1`, `edgesynology2`) and gives the operator:
6	
7	- live telemetry (metrics, logs, storage, processes, network, containers)
8	- grouped issues with persistent conversation threads
9	- a 3-stage LLM-driven issue agent that diagnoses problems and proposes fixes
10	- a controlled approval gate for any state-modifying action on the NAS
11	
12	Focus is Synology Drive / ShareSync reliability, file operation visibility, sync
13	and replication failures, storage and I/O attribution, and silent task/backup
14	failures.
15	
16	## Component map
17	
18	```
19	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
20	│ NAS (×2: edgesynology1, edgesynology2)                      │
21	│  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────────┐   │
22	│  │  agent (Go)          │  │  nas-api (Go)              │   │
23	│  │  polls DSM APIs      │  │  3-tier shell executor     │   │
24	│  │  reads /proc, /sys   │  │  port 7734                 │   │
25	│  │  watches logs        │  │  tier 1: read-only (auto)  │   │
26	│  │  → SQLite WAL        │  │  tier 2: service ops       │   │
27	│  └──────────────────────┘  │  tier 3: file ops          │   │
28	│           │                 └────────────┬───────────────┘   │
29	│           │ PostgREST (batch flush)       │ HTTPS/Tailscale   │
30	└───────────┼───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┘
31	            ▼                               ▼
32	┌───────────────────┐    ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
33	│ Supabase          │◄───│ web app (Next.js)                  │
34	│ qnjimovrsaacneqk  │    │ mon.designflow.app                 │
35	│ 53 tables         │    │  - issue detector                  │
36	│ partitioned +     │    │  - 3-stage issue-agent pipeline    │
37	│ time-series       │    │  - operator approval UI            │
38	└───────────────────┘    │  - copilot chat                    │
39	                         └───────────────────────────────────┘
40	                                        ▲
41	                              MCP / Streamable HTTP
42	                         ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
43	                         │ nas-mcp (Node.js)                  │
44	                         │ nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp         │
45	                         │ 119-definition registry (lazy-load)      │
46	                         │ 7 small always-on tools/session    │
47	                         └───────────────────────────────────┘
48	```
49	
50	Everything is deployed via push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds and pushes four
51	Docker images to GHCR. The web app and nas-mcp redeploy automatically via Coolify
52	webhooks; the agent and nas-api are picked up by Watchtower on each NAS within
53	~5 minutes. There is one branch: `main`.
54	
55	---
56	
57	## Agent (`apps/agent`)
58	
59	The Go agent runs on each NAS as a Docker container with `network_mode: host`.
60	It collects telemetry on a rolling schedule, buffers every write in a local SQLite
61	WAL at `/app/data/wal.db`, and flushes to Supabase in per-table batches every 30s
62	via PostgREST.
63	
64	### Volume mounts
65	
66	The NAS host filesystem is exposed to the agent container under `/host/` to avoid
67	shadowing the container's own kernel namespaces:
68	
69	| Host path | Container path | Used by |
70	|---|---|---|
71	| `/proc` | `/host/proc` | process stats, mdstat, diskstats |
72	| `/sys` | `/host/sys` | cgroup I/O, btrfs counters, thermal |
73	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` | logwatcher, backup logs |
74	| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` | drive log collector |
75	| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | sharesync log collector |
76	
77	### Collector inventory
78	
79	| Collector | File | Primary tables | Default interval |
80	|---|---|---|---|
81	| system | `collector/system.go` | `metrics`, `container_status`, `storage_snapshots` | 30s / 60s |
82	| drive | `collector/drive.go` | `sync_task_snapshots`, `drive_team_folders`, `nas_logs` | 30s |
83	| sharesync | `collector/sharesync.go` | `alerts`, `nas_logs` | 5m (hardcoded) |
84	| sharehealth | `collector/sharehealth.go` | `nas_logs`, `metrics`, `package_status`, `dsm_errors` | 2m |
85	| storagepool | `collector/storagepool.go` | `snapshot_replicas`, `metrics`, `nas_logs` | 60s (mdstat) / 5m (replicas) |
86	| process | `collector/process.go` | `process_snapshots` | 15s |
87	| diskstats | `collector/diskstats.go` | `disk_io_stats`, `metrics` (`disk_inflight_ios`) | 15s |
88	| container_io | `collector/container_io.go` | `container_io` | 30s |
89	| connections | `collector/connections.go` | `net_connections` | 30s |
90	| sysextras | `collector/sysextras.go` | `metrics` (cpu_iowait_pct, thermal, memory, NFS, VM pressure, btrfs errors) | 30s |
91	| services | `collector/services.go` | `service_health`, `nas_logs` | 60s |
92	| hyperbackup | `collector/hyperbackup.go` | `backup_tasks`, `nas_logs` | 5m |
93	| schedtasks | `collector/schedtasks.go` | `scheduled_tasks`, `nas_logs` | 5m |
94	| infra | `collector/infra.go` | `metrics`, `nas_logs` | 2m |
95	| custom | `collector/custom.go` | `custom_metric_data` | polls DB every 60s |
96	| logwatcher | `logwatcher/watcher.go` | `nas_logs` | 10s tail interval |
97	| security | `security/watcher.go` | `security_events` | inotify-driven |
98	
99	All collector goroutines use the `wg.Add(1)` / `defer wg.Done()` WaitGroup
100	pattern in `cmd/agent/main.go`. Omitting this drops in-flight WAL writes on
101	shutdown (the ShareSync collector had this bug; see AGENTS.md §10).
102	
103	### Notable collectors
104	
105	**ShareSync health (`collector/sharesync.go`)** — scans `dscc.log` and
106	`dscc_monitor.log` directly at
107	`/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync/log/` using byte-offset watermarks stored
108	in the WAL's `checkpoints` table. Detects three failure patterns invisible to the
109	DSM UI: queue jams (same path in `RedoEvent`/`PullEvent` ≥5 times without a
110	`DoneEvent`), basis-file corruption (`PrepareDownloadFile` repeat without
111	`DoneEvent`), and transport flaps (≥3 error-code-26 / daemon-socket / reconnect
112	events in a window).
113	
114	**Storagepool (`collector/storagepool.go`)** — reads `/host/proc/mdstat` directly
115	every 60s for RAID scrub/rebuild/check progress. Also emits `disk_inflight_ios`
116	(instantaneous in-progress I/O count per device from `/proc/diskstats` field 9).
117	Alerts once on healthy→degraded state transitions.
118	
119	**Log watcher (`logwatcher/watcher.go`)** — tails the following sources by default:
120	
121	| Container path | Source label |
122	|---|---|
123	| `/host/log/messages` | `system` |
124	| `/host/log/synolog/synobackup.log` | `backup` |
125	| `/host/log/synologydrive.log` | `drive_server` |
126	| `/host/log/synolog/synosecurity.log` | `security` |
127	| `/host/log/synolog/synoconnection.log` | `connection` |
128	| `/host/log/synolog/synopkg.log` | `package` |
129	| `/host/log/samba/log.smbd` | `smb` |
130	| `/host/log/synolog/synowebapi.log` | `webapi` |
131	| `/host/log/synolog/synostorage.log` | `storage` |
132	| `/host/log/synolog/synoshare.log` | `share` |
133	| `/host/log/kern.log` | `kernel` |
134	| `/host/log/synolog/synoinfo.log` | `system_info` |
135	| `/host/log/synolog/synoservice.log` | `service` |
136	| `/host/shares/@synologydrive/log/*.log` | `drive` |
137	| `/host/shares/@synologydrive/log/syncfolder.log` | `drive_sharesync` |
138	
139	The `@synologydrive` paths are mounted at `/host/shares/@synologydrive` (not under
140	`/host/volume1`); `inferDriveLogFiles` prepends `/host/shares` first.
141	
142	**Custom collector (`collector/custom.go`)** — polls `custom_metric_schedules`
143	every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and executes the scheduled
144	shell command. Output is stored in `custom_metric_data`. Used for ad-hoc deep
145	diagnostics and permanent nightly schedules (e.g. `nightly_disk_health` from
146	migration 00040, which runs at 2am UTC on weekdays and collects `/proc/diskstats`,
147	`/proc/mdstat`, and per-device inflight IOs).
148	
149	### WAL and sender (`sender/sender.go`)
150	
151	The SQLite WAL at `/app/data/wal.db` holds two logical stores: per-table row
152	queues (flushed to Supabase every 30s) and a `checkpoints` table (durable named
153	string values such as log file byte offsets and collector cursors).
154	
155	**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx` batch failure, the sender
156	re-sends each row individually so good rows land and only the bad row accumulates
157	retries. This prevents a single malformed row from silently freezing an entire
158	telemetry stream (as happened for ~19h/23d before the fix — see incidents).
159	
160	**`package_status` is the only merge-duplicates upsert.** Every other table is
161	append-only inserts. The WAL is capped at `MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB);
162	oldest entries are dropped when the cap is reached.
163	
164	---
165	
166	## NAS API (`apps/nas-api`)
167	
168	Runs on each NAS as a Docker container on port 7734 (`network_mode: host`,
169	`pid: host`). Accepts bearer-authenticated POST requests from the web app and
170	nas-mcp to classify and execute shell commands.
171	
172	The service exposes three HTTP endpoints:
173	
174	| Method + Path | Purpose |
175	|---|---|
176	| `GET /health` | Liveness check; returns build SHA and time |
177	| `POST /preview` | Classify a command's tier without executing it |
178	| `POST /exec` | Execute a command; requires tier + optional approval token |
179	
180	It also exposes a native (non-shell) **file-inventory job** API (Phase 1 of the
181	archive work — see [synology-archive.md](synology-archive.md)): `POST/GET
182	/jobs/inventory`, `GET /jobs/inventory/{id}`, `…/{id}/result`,
183	`POST /jobs/inventory/schedule`, and `…/{id}/cancel`. These run an in-process Go
184	`filepath.WalkDir` scanner (`internal/jobs/`), persist state to the durable
185	`/app/data/jobs` mount, and gate state-changing ops with the same HMAC tier-2
186	approval token as `/exec` (signed over a canonical op string, not a shell command).
187	They do **not** pass through the validator or `/exec`.
188	
189	Phase 2 adds a staged, reversible **archive-move** API under
190	`/jobs/archive-move/*` (`plan`, `{id}`, `{id}/manifest`, `{id}/result`,
191	`{id}/execute`, `{id}/cancel`, `{id}/rollback`, `{id}/verify`). The move state
192	machine (plan → preflight → snapshot → execute → verify → rollback, plus a
193	`clean_empty_dirs` mode) relocates cutoff-qualified files, or selected-root files
194	when `force_archive` is explicitly enabled, into `<share>/Archive` by atomic
195	**rename within the same Btrfs subvolume** (verifying inode/size/mtime/btime per
196	file, rolling back any mismatch). `protect_newer_than` still blocks force-mode
197	candidates. The executor takes a read-only Btrfs snapshot before any
198	write, and records every file and pruned directory in a JSONL manifest for
199	reversibility. It writes via the **writable `/btrfs/volume1/<share>`** mount (the
200	per-share `/volume1/<share>` mounts stay read-only); `execute` and `rollback` are
201	**tier 3**. Btrfs subvolume/snapshot calls sit behind an injectable interface so
202	the logic is unit-tested on temp trees.
203	
204	### Three-tier execution model
205	
206	| Tier | Label | Approval required | Examples |
207	|---|---|---|---|
208	| 1 | read-only | No — auto-executes | `cat /proc/mdstat`, `df -h`, `smartctl -a`, `tail -n 100 /var/log/kern.log` |
209	| 2 | service ops | HMAC approval token | `synopkg restart SynologyDrive`, DSM WebAPI `SYNO.Docker.Container` start/stop for the monitor agent |
210	| 3 | file ops | HMAC approval token | `mv /volume1/file.old /volume1/file`, `btrfs scrub start /volume1` |
211	
212	### Validator (`internal/validator/validator.go`)
213	
214	`ClassifyTier(command)` returns the minimum required tier. `Validate(command, tier)`
215	enforces that the requested tier is sufficient and that no hard-block patterns match.
216	
217	### DSM Container Manager lifecycle
218	
219	What changed:
220	Monitor-agent container status/actions exposed by the web dashboard and tool
221	catalogs use DSM Container Manager WebAPI (`SYNO.Docker.Container`) rather than
222	`docker compose`/`docker stop`/`docker start`.
223	
224	Why:
225	Container lifecycle changes made directly through Docker CLI can leave Synology
226	Container Manager's GUI state stale or incorrect. The monitor UI should match
227	the backend/DSM source of truth.
228	
229	Future sessions should:
230	Keep dashboard/tool lifecycle actions on DSM WebAPI paths. `docker compose` is
231	blocked by the NAS API validator for routine service actions; use it only for the
232	documented exceptional deployment/configuration cases where compose state itself
233	must be applied.
234	
235	**Hard-blocked regardless of tier:** disk destruction (`mkfs`, `fdisk`, `parted`,
236	`dd if=`, `wipefs`), root filesystem destruction (`rm -rf /`), DSM binary writes,
237	`synopkg install/uninstall`, firmware writes (`flash_eraseall`, `nandwrite`,
238	`insmod`, `rmmod`), ptrace code-injection (`gdb`, `lldb`), destructive `hdparm`
239	flags (`--security-`, `-y`, `-Y`, `-W`), user account changes (`useradd`,
240	`userdel`, `passwd <user>`), shutdown/reboot/poweroff, package managers
241	(`apt-get install`, `opkg install`, `pip install`), recursive grep on
242	`@synologydrive`/`@SynologyDriveShareSync` (a 4-day runaway grep incident on
243	production triggered this — see AGENTS.md §13), unrestricted Docker host-control
244	commands (`docker run/create`, arbitrary compose lifecycle, etc.).
245	
246	**Tier 1 enforcement:** `writePatterns` (regex list) is checked; any match elevates
247	to tier 2+. Real output redirection (`> file`, not `>/dev/null` or `2>&1`) is also
248	a write.
249	
250	The validator is compiled by Go's standard `regexp` package, so every regex must
251	be valid RE2 syntax. Lookahead, lookbehind, and backreferences are not supported.
252	Do not use patterns such as `(?!...)` in `regexp.MustCompile`; an invalid pattern
253	panics at process startup and prevents the NAS API from binding port 7734.
254	
255	**Tier 2 enforcement:** commands touching `/volume*/` paths outside the monitor
256	stack are elevated to tier 3.
257	
258	**HMAC approval tokens** are minted fresh by the web app at execution time in
259	`pipeline-v2.ts::executeApprovedAction` and `nas-api-client.ts::buildNasApiApprovalToken`.
260	They are never persisted; the 15-minute expiry bounds only the exec→exec window,
261	not the operator's think time. Persisting tokens would cause 403s after expiry.
262	
263	**Container capabilities:** `apparmor=unconfined` (DSM rejects the default AppArmor
264	profile on container init), `SYS_ADMIN` (required for btrfs subvolume, scrub, and
265	snapshot operations), `SYS_PTRACE` (enables `strace` for D-state process diagnosis;
266	`gdb`/`lldb` are hard-blocked by the validator to prevent code injection).
267	
268	**Executor process group kill:** `executor.go` sets `Setpgid: true` and uses
269	`syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` with a `WaitDelay: 2s`. This kills the entire process
270	group on timeout, not just the bash parent — preventing orphaned subprocesses
271	(e.g. a `grep` forked by `grep ... | head`).
272	
273	---
274	
275	## NAS MCP (`apps/nas-mcp`)
276	
277	FastMCP Node.js server at `nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp`. AI chat clients
278	(claude.ai, Claude Desktop) connect over Streamable HTTP. The server is
279	**fully stateless** (`transportType: "httpStream"`, `stateless: true`) and does
280	not rely on persistent `mcp-session-id` state. This eliminates stale-session
281	problems after redeploys and avoids the 4-minute hang class that stateful
282	transport caused via the claude.ai proxy.
283	
284	### Tool surface
285	
286	Seven small tools are registered eagerly on every request:
287	
288	| Always-on tool | Purpose |
289	|---|---|
290	| `list_capabilities({ group, safety, limit })` | Browse enabled operations by group/safety without invoking anything |
291	| `get_capability_details({ name })` | Return one operation's full contract, examples, safety metadata, and related tools |
292	| `tool_search({ query, limit })` | Search the 119-definition registry by keyword; returns names, descriptions, safety class, groups, parameter shapes, and exact `invoke_tool` call shape as text |
293	| `invoke_tool({ name, target, args })` | Execute any registry tool by name |
294	| `run_command({ target, command })` | Free-form tier-1-only shell command |
295	| `check_disk_space({ target })` | Disk and inode usage across all volumes |
296	| `restart_nas_api({ target, confirmed })` | Restart the NAS API container |
297	
298	The full 119-definition registry is in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` (the
299	`ALL_TOOL_DEFS` array). Clients browse with `list_capabilities`, inspect one
300	operation with `get_capability_details`, search with `tool_search`, and execute
301	with `invoke_tool`. The registry is never loaded eagerly — loading all 119 schemas
302	put ~50k tokens into every session and degraded it after ~10–15 tool calls.
303	FastMCP session-level instructions tell clients to browse/search/detail before
304	most NAS tasks and then call `invoke_tool` with the exact returned operation name.
305	
306	Tool enablement is controlled by `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`. A tool present
307	in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `enabled_read_tools` or `enabled_write_tools` is
308	rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. Adding a new always-on tool
309	requires adding it to the `EAGER_TOOLS` constant in `src/index.ts`.
310	
311	### NAS API filesystem view used by tools
312	
313	Predefined NAS tools run inside the `synology-monitor-nas-api` container, not in
314	the telemetry agent container. The most important mounts from
315	`deploy/synology/docker-compose.agent.yml` are:
316	
317	| Host path | NAS API path | Notes |
318	|---|---|---|
319	| `/usr/syno` | `/host/usr/syno` | DSM binaries/config |
320	| `/var/packages` | `/host/packages` | DSM package state; not `/host/var/packages` |
321	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` | DSM logs |
322	| `/volume1` | `/btrfs/volume1` | Full Btrfs volume for subvolume/snapshot commands |
323	| selected shares | `/volume1/<share>` | Narrow read-only shared-folder mounts |
324	
325	This is why snapshot tools check `/btrfs/volumeN`, and DSM/package inspectors check
326	`/host/packages`. Read-only tools may also use DSM WebAPI `list`/`query` methods
327	when SQLite/config files are hidden by DSM 7 package layout.
328	
329	### Approval flow for write tools
330	
331	For tier-2/3 write tools, `invoke_tool` calls `POST /preview` on the NAS API first.
332	If the command requires approval and `args.confirmed` is not `true`, the tool
333	returns a plain-text preview of the command. The client must call `invoke_tool`
334	again with `confirmed: true`. On confirmation, `src/index.ts::buildApprovalToken`
335	mints the HMAC token and passes it to `POST /exec`.
336	
337	### Network and timeout architecture
338	
339	- `Connection: close` on every nas-api request — prevents undici pool exhaustion
340	  when timed-out requests do not return their socket (sub-ms Tailscale RTT makes
341	  re-handshake cost negligible).
342	- Tool deadline: 45s hard cap per `invoke_tool` call, implemented in
343	  `withToolDeadline`. Returns a clear timeout error rather than holding the
344	  connection until Claude's 4-minute client timeout.
345	- `/exec` AbortController: 25s command + 5s buffer = 30s, with `timeout_ms: 25000`
346	  sent to the NAS API so it kills the subprocess first.
347	
348	---
349	
350	## Web app (`apps/web`)
351	
352	Next.js 14 app at `mon.designflow.app`. Uses Supabase for auth and data. Server
353	components and API routes call Supabase with the service role key via
354	`createAdminClient()`. Client-side components use the anon key.
355	
356	`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are baked into the
357	client bundle at `docker build` time via build args in `web-image.yml`. Changing
358	them in Coolify after the image is built has no effect.
359	
360	Key subsystems:
361	
362	| File | Purpose |
363	|---|---|
364	| `lib/server/issue-detector.ts` | Fingerprints alerts + logs into issues by family |
365	| `lib/server/ai/pipeline-v2.ts` | 3-stage pipeline orchestrator, one job per turn |
366	| `lib/server/issue-workflow.ts` | Job queue and status state machine |
367	| `lib/server/issue-agent.ts` | `gatherTelemetryContext` — queries 13+ tables in parallel |
368	| `lib/server/nas-api-client.ts` | HTTP client for nas-api; mints HMAC tokens |
369	| `lib/server/ai-settings.ts` | Model selection + fallback chain from `ai_settings` table |
370	| `lib/server/copilot.ts` | Legacy NAS assistant chat (OpenRouter/MiniMax) |
371	
372	The issue worker mode is controlled by `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` (`inline` = drains on
373	API requests; `background` = dedicated loop). The worker polls `issue_jobs` and
374	calls `runIssueAgentV2` for each pending job.
375	
376	---
377	
378	## 3-stage AI issue-agent pipeline
379	
380	The only active pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and
381	OpenRouter inference path were removed. Entrypoint: `pipeline-v2.ts::runIssueAgentV2`.
382	
383	One invocation of `runIssueAgentV2` is one job = one resumable step:
384	
385	```
386	runIssueAgentV2(supabase, userId, issueId)
387	  │
388	  ├─ 1. executeApprovedAction — execute any pending tier-2/3 action,
389	  │      persist result to issue_evidence_items, post system message
390	  │
391	  ├─ 2. Stage 1 (first turn only) — gather telemetry, dedupe, persist
392	  │      lossless set to issue_evidence_items
393	  │
394	  ├─ 3. Stage 2 — one agentic reasoning turn with read-only tool loop
395	  │      → one of: continue | propose_remediation | ask_user |
396	  │                blocked_on_issue | resolved | stuck
397	  │
398	  └─ 4. Stage 3 (on resolved/stuck) — operator message + durable memories
399	```
400	
401	### Stage 1 — Lossless Structurer (`ai/stage1-structurer.ts`)
402	
403	Deterministic, no model call. Runs once per issue on the first turn.
404	
405	**Input:** raw telemetry from `gatherTelemetryContext` (`issue-agent.ts`), which
406	queries these tables in parallel: `alerts`, `nas_logs` (general + high-signal
407	sources), `process_snapshots`, `disk_io_stats`, `scheduled_tasks`, `backup_tasks`,
408	`snapshot_replicas`, `container_io`, `sync_task_snapshots`, `metrics`,
409	`storage_snapshots`, `dsm_errors`.
410	
411	**Processing:** `telemetryToRawItems` maps each table to a `RawEvidenceItem` using
412	the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` map. For structured rows (process snapshots, container I/O,
413	disk I/O), the full JSON of the row becomes the evidence `body` — no fields are
414	stripped into metadata, ensuring `cpu_pct`, `state`, `read_bps`, `write_bps`, and
415	`queue_depth` are visible to Stage 2.
416	
417	`dedupeEvidence` collapses byte-identical `(source, body)` pairs into groups with
418	`{ dedup_count, first_ts, last_ts }`. Each distinct event is kept verbatim.
419	
420	`classifyEvidence` marks each item `in_scope` (matches `affected_nas` or is
421	severity ≥ error) and `anomalous` (severity ≥ error, or warning with a state-change
422	keyword like "failed", "timeout", "crash").
423	
424	**Output:**
425	1. Full deduped set persisted to `issue_evidence_items` (lossless store,
426	   idempotent — deletes prior rows for the issue first).
427	2. A bounded prioritized evidence slice for the Stage 2 prompt:
428	   - Budget: 12,000 tokens / 48,000 chars.
429	   - 70% of budget: in-scope/anomalous events rendered verbatim.
430	   - 30% of budget: high-volume noise rendered as dedup-with-count summaries.
431	   - Always includes the evidence index (source × hour bucket, sorted by event count).
432	   - If the priority set exceeds budget, a visible truncation notice is appended
433	     (never silent).
434	
435	Stage 2 can retrieve any item from the lossless store at any time via
436	`fetch_evidence`, which reads `issue_evidence_items` — not the raw tables.
437	
438	**Why Stage 1 is deterministic:** the previous issue-agent compressed logs with a
439	model before the reasoner saw them. That made the pipeline lossy: raw log messages
440	were shortened, repeated-but-distinct events were summarized away, and the
441	reasoning stages could miss the evidence that would falsify a bad hypothesis.
442	Stage 1 deliberately preserves every distinct `(source, body)` item and only
443	deduplicates byte-identical repetitions. The prompt slice is bounded, but the DB
444	store is the lossless source of truth.
445	
446	### Stage 2 — Reasoning Core (`ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`)
447	
448	One agentic turn per job invocation. Model: operator-configured via `ai_settings`
449	table (`stage_reasoning_model`); default `claude-sonnet-4-6` / high effort.
450	
451	**Context is rebuilt from the DB on every turn** (resumable invariant): the worker
452	that picks up a job may be a different process after an approval gate or restart.
453	Three parallel loads:
454	
455	1. `getReasoningConfig()` — model + effort from `ai_settings`
456	2. `loadEvidenceSlice(supabase, issueId)` — reads `issue_evidence_items`, rebuilds the
457	   bounded slice without re-running ingestion
458	3. `buildWholeSystemSnapshot(supabase, userId, issue)` — parallel: active alerts
459	   (6h), open issues, NAS reachability probe (3s timeout to primary NAS)
460	4. `loadTranscript(supabase, userId, issueId)` — last 60 `issue_messages` rows
461	   mapped to `{role, content}` pairs
462	
463	**Prompt assembly (stable → dynamic for cache correctness):**
464	
465	| Block | Stability | Content |
466	|---|---|---|
467	| `system` | stable | `SYSTEM_PROMPT` constant — operating rules, tool inventory |
468	| `output_schema` | stable | `OUTPUT_SCHEMA` constant — required JSON fields and decision values |
469	| `taxonomy` | stable | `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant — issue families, iowait interpretation guide, DSM blind spots |
470	| `snapshot` | semi-stable | NAS reachability, active alert counts, open sibling issues |
471	| `issue` | semi-stable | Issue title, severity, status, affected_nas, current hypothesis |
472	| `evidence` | dynamic | Bounded evidence slice from `loadEvidenceSlice` |
473	| `instruction` | dynamic | Per-turn instruction; includes re-chew guard text when triggered |
474	
475	Stable blocks are passed to `context-compiler.ts::block.stable()`, which marks them
476	for prefix caching. Only the last two blocks change per turn.
477	
478	**Tool catalog available to Stage 2 (all tier-1, auto-execute):**
479	
480	- `fetch_evidence` — queries `issue_evidence_items` for this issue; aggregation
481	  (`group_by`) or pagination (`page`, `filter`). Works when NAS is offline.
482	- `run_command` — free-form shell command validated at tier 1 by nas-api. Results
483	  are persisted to `issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:run_command"`.
484	- All `write:false` tools from `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` (packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts)
485	  with the `target` parameter removed from the schema (it is inferred from
486	  `issue.affected_nas[0]`). Tool results are also persisted to
487	  `issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:<name>"`.
488	
489	Write tools (`def.write === true`) are excluded entirely from Stage 2's tool
490	catalog. The model proposes write actions via the `propose_remediation` terminal;
491	the operator approves them; `executeApprovedAction` in `pipeline-v2.ts` runs them
492	on the next job invocation.
493	
494	**Tool-use discipline:** Stage 2 should prefer predefined read-only tools when
495	they cover the question because they have tuned command shapes and timeouts. Use
496	`run_command` only for precise read-only probes that are not covered by the catalog.
497	Use `fetch_evidence` aggregation first (`group_by`) before paging raw rows when
498	the evidence store may be large. Do not re-run a tool whose result is already in
499	`issue_evidence_items` unless the live value is expected to have changed and the
500	new sample is diagnostically meaningful.
501	
502	**Re-chew guard:** each turn, Stage 2 hashes the evidence slice text. If the hash
503	matches the previous turn's hash and `decision=continue` is returned again, a
504	`rechew_repeat` counter increments in `issue.metadata`. After ≥2 repeats the
505	per-turn instruction becomes a re-chew guard message, and if the model still returns
506	`continue` after 2 repeats, `toTurnOutcome` forces `decision=ask_user`.
507	
508	**Turn cap:** `TURN_CAP` (from `stage2-turn.ts`) bounds the total number of turns.
509	If exceeded, Stage 2 returns `stuck` without calling the model.
510	
511	**Terminal mapping** (`applyTurnOutcome` in `stage2-turn.ts`):
512	
513	| `decision` | Outcome | Issue status |
514	|---|---|---|
515	| `continue` | enqueue next job | `running` |
516	| `propose_remediation` | persist `issue_actions` row with command/tier/target/summary | `waiting_for_approval` |
517	| `ask_user` | post `issue_messages` with question | `waiting_on_user` |
518	| `blocked_on_issue` | record dependency | `waiting_on_issue` |
519	| `resolved` | close issue; trigger Stage 3 | `resolved` |
520	| `stuck` | close issue; trigger Stage 3 | `stuck` |
521	
522	**Model fit:** Stage 2 needs the strongest reasoning model in the pipeline. The
523	important capabilities are reliable tool calling, long-context coherence,
524	structured JSON output, calibrated uncertainty, and the ability to form and test a
525	ranked hypothesis across several resumable turns. Low-latency or prose quality is
526	less important than converging in a small number of turns without proposing unsafe
527	or vague remediations. Domain familiarity with Linux storage, Btrfs, `/proc`, and
528	Synology DSM helps, but the taxonomy prompt is intended to make a strong general
529	reasoner effective.
530	
531	### Stage 3 — Explainer / Memory (`ai/stage3-explainer.ts`)
532	
533	Single-shot, cheap, low-effort. Model: `stage_explainer_model` from `ai_settings`;
534	default `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` / low effort. Runs after Stage 2 reaches
535	`resolved` or `stuck`. Best-effort — a Stage 3 failure never fails the resolution.
536	
537	**Input:** two parallel reads from Supabase:
538	- `issue_evidence_items` where `in_scope=true`, ordered by `ts` desc, limit 30
539	- `issue_actions` for this issue, limit 20 (command, summary, status, result excerpt)
540	
541	**Prompt:** three blocks:
542	- `system` (stable): "write the closing operator update and extract durable knowledge"
543	- `output_schema` (stable): JSON schema for `operator_message` and `memories[]`
544	- `issue_context` (dynamic): JSON blob of issue title, final hypothesis, conversation
545	  summary, evidence highlights, and action results
546	
547	**Output:**
548	1. `operator_message` (2–5 sentences, plain language) posted to `issue_messages` as
549	   role `agent`.
550	2. Up to 5 `agent_memory` entries inserted into `agent_memory` table with
551	   `source_issue_id`. Valid `memory_type` values: `nas_profile`, `issue_pattern`,
552	   `calibration`, `institutional`. Empty memories are skipped. `nas_id: null` means
553	   the lesson is universal across both NASes.
554	
555	Memories are loaded at the start of Stage 2 investigations via
556	`agent-memory-store.ts::loadMemoriesForIssue`, classified by subject so only
557	relevant topics are included (e.g. HyperBackup memories for backup issues).
558	
559	### Stage model selection (live, de-curated)
560	
561	The per-stage model dropdowns in the AI Stages settings panel are populated
562	**live** from every connected provider — a provider counts as connected when its
563	API key env (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`,
564	`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`, `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY`) is present. `provider-models.ts` calls
565	each connected provider's list-models endpoint (cached in-process ~10 min) and the
566	`/api/ai-models` route serves the union to the UI. `MODEL_CATALOG` in
567	`packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` is no longer the menu — it is a
568	precise-metadata **override** (hand-verified effort/cache/tool-use per model) and
569	the offline/no-keys fallback.
570	
571	At runtime, `callModel` resolves a model id through a three-step chain
572	(`resolveModelDescriptor` → `resolveLiveDescriptor`):
573	
574	1. **catalog** — exact match in `MODEL_CATALOG` (verified metadata),
575	2. **derived** — for catalog-miss ids, a descriptor inferred from the id (provider
576	   by prefix; cache style per provider; effort/tool-use by conservative id
577	   patterns),
578	3. **live map** — for off-pattern ids (e.g. third-party models hosted on
579	   DashScope), the provider recovered from the live provider→models map.
580	
581	It only throws when no connected provider offers the id. A selected model whose
582	capabilities are *derived* (not catalog-backed) is flagged with an amber "inferred
583	model" warning in the UI, because its effort knob and tool-use support are
584	best-guess until a catalog row is added. Capability gating per stage (Stage 2
585	requires tool use; all stages require structured output) still applies to the live
586	list.
587	
588	**Model fit:** Stage 3 is a communication and memory-distillation task, not a
589	diagnostic task. It benefits from clear writing, good compression, and the ability
590	to extract specific reusable lessons. It does not need live tools, long multi-turn
591	state, or high reasoning effort. A cheaper model is acceptable because Stage 3 is
592	best-effort and must never undo Stage 2's terminal outcome.
593

595	
596	## Data flow summary
597	
598	```
599	NAS hardware / DSM APIs
600	        │
601	        │ (each collector at its interval)
602	        ▼
603	agent collectors (Go) ──────────────────────────────┐
604	        │ Queue* methods                             │
605	        ▼                                            │
606	SQLite WAL (/app/data/wal.db)                        │
607	        │ sender.go flush every 30s                  │
608	        │ PostgREST batch insert                     │
609	        ▼                                            │
610	Supabase telemetry tables                            │
611	  metrics, nas_logs, disk_io_stats,                  │
612	  process_snapshots, backup_tasks, ...               │
613	        │                                            │
614	        │ issue-detector.ts (fingerprint)            │
615	        ▼                                            │
616	issues table (one row per grouped problem)           │
617	        │                                            │
618	        │ pipeline-v2.ts job                         │
619	        ▼                                            │
620	Stage 1: gatherTelemetryContext ─────────────────────┘
621	  → telemetryToRawItems
622	  → dedupeEvidence + classifyEvidence
623	  → persistEvidenceItems (issue_evidence_items)
624	  → buildEvidenceSlice
625	        │
626	        ▼
627	Stage 2: runStage2Turn (agentic loop)
628	  context: evidence slice + transcript + snapshot
629	  tools: fetch_evidence, run_command, 100+ NAS tools
630	  each tool result → issue_evidence_items
631	  terminal → issue_actions (remediation) or status change
632	        │
633	        │ (on resolved/stuck)
634	        ▼
635	Stage 3: runStage3Explainer (single-shot)
636	  → operator_message → issue_messages
637	  → agent_memory entries
638	```
639	
640	---
641	
642	## Database
643	
644	Supabase project `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn`. 53 tables total (migrations 00001–00041).
645	
646	**Partitioned tables** (pg_partman, monthly, auto-retention): `metrics`,
647	`nas_logs`, `storage_snapshots`, `container_status`, `drive_activities`.
648	
649	**Key table groups:**
650	
651	| Group | Tables |
652	|---|---|
653	| Telemetry | `metrics`, `nas_logs`, `disk_io_stats`, `process_snapshots`, `net_connections`, `container_io`, `backup_tasks`, `scheduled_tasks`, `snapshot_replicas`, `service_health`, `dsm_errors`, `package_status`, `security_events`, `drive_activities`, `sync_task_snapshots`, `drive_team_folders` |
654	| Issue pipeline | `issues`, `issue_messages`, `issue_evidence`, `issue_evidence_items`, `issue_actions`, `issue_jobs`, `issue_stage_runs`, `issue_state_transitions`, `agent_memory` |
655	| AI config | `ai_settings`, `ai_model_calls` |
656	| Copilot / resolution | `copilot_sessions`, `copilot_messages`, `copilot_actions`, `sync_remediations` |
657	| Custom collection | `custom_metric_schedules`, `custom_metric_data` |
658	
659	**`issue_evidence` vs `issue_evidence_items` — different tables, different purposes:**
660	
661	- `issue_evidence` (migration 00022): curated human-readable notes (title/detail)
662	  written by the copilot, the resolution API, and `seedIssueFromOrigin`. Not used
663	  by the 3-stage pipeline.
664	- `issue_evidence_items` (migration 00038): the lossless telemetry store for the
665	  3-stage pipeline. Written by Stage 1 and by every Stage 2 tool call. Read by
666	  Stage 2 `fetch_evidence` and Stage 3. Do not query the wrong one.
667	
668	**`package_status`** is the only merge-duplicates upsert (current state, one row
669	per NAS+package). All other telemetry tables are append-only inserts.
670	
671	**AI settings** (`ai_settings` table) must be read via `createAdminClient()`
672	(service role). The issue agent runs as a background worker with no user session;
673	the session client returns empty under RLS, silently falling back to hardcoded
674	defaults.
675	
676	---
677	
678	## Key design constraints and intentional decisions
679	
680	### No source whitelists on `nas_logs` / `alerts`
681	
682	`CHECK` constraints that enumerated allowed source values were dropped in migration
683	00035. They had to be hand-expanded every time a collector added a source, and one
684	bad row failing the whole PostgREST batch silently froze ingestion for ~19h/23d.
685	The agent governs what it writes; the sender isolates bad rows.
686	
687	### NAS MCP is fully stateless (FastMCP HTTP Stream)
688	
689	`apps/nas-mcp` uses TypeScript FastMCP with `transportType: "httpStream"` and
690	`stateless: true`. It does not depend on persistent `mcp-session-id` state across
691	requests or redeploys. Stateful mode brings back session-resume failures after
692	Coolify restarts, and the claude.ai proxy's old 4-minute hang class was traced to
693	stateful transport behavior.
694	
695	### NAS MCP exposes 7 small tools but has a 119-definition registry
696	
697	Pre-loading 119 schemas puts ~50k tokens into every session and degrades it after
698	~10–15 calls. Lazy-load via catalog/search/detail + `invoke_tool` keeps the
699	always-on surface compact. `notifications/tools/list_changed` is not used because
700	Claude clients cache the initial `tools/list` and do not re-fetch on the
701	notification.
702	
703	### HMAC approval tokens are never persisted
704	
705	Stage 2 persists the action intent (command, tier, target, summary) in
706	`issue_actions` but never the HMAC token. The token is minted fresh in
707	`pipeline-v2.ts::executeApprovedAction` at execution time. Persisting tokens would
708	cause 403s after the 15-minute expiry; the operator's approval window is often hours.
709	
710	### Stage 2 tool results go into `issue_evidence_items`
711	
712	Every `run_command` and predefined NAS tool call in Stage 2 inserts a row into
713	`issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:<name>"`. This means later turns can
714	page the result via `fetch_evidence` without re-running the command — and the
715	Stage 3 explainer sees the full investigative trail without needing a separate
716	transcript of tool outputs.
717	
718	### Prompt order: stable → dynamic
719	
720	`context-compiler.ts` enforces that system/output-schema/taxonomy blocks come
721	before snapshot/issue/evidence/instruction blocks. This keeps the cacheable prefix
722	maximally long (stable blocks are identical across all issues and all turns), so
723	only the evidence slice and per-turn instruction incur full token costs.
724	
725	### Provider cache observability matters
726	
727	The issue agent calls providers directly so provider-specific cache controls and
728	usage fields remain visible. Do not route the cached inference path through an
729	aggregator that flattens request/response shapes. A thin wrapper for timeouts,
730	retry classification, and normalized accounting is fine, but raw provider usage
731	must still be persisted or inspectable.
732	
733	Provider-specific details that have caused bugs in similar systems:
734	- Anthropic explicit cache reads/writes are reported as
735	  `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens`; extended thinking
736	  requires the provider's required temperature shape.
737	- OpenAI caches stable prefixes automatically; cache reads surface under
738	  `prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens`.
739	- DeepSeek's cache fields are `prompt_cache_hit_tokens` and
740	  `prompt_cache_miss_tokens`, not the OpenAI names.
741	- Qwen/DashScope multi-turn cache behavior depends on preserving the provider's
742	  response/session id between turns.
743	- Gemini explicit cached content, if used, must have a tracked lifecycle so paid
744	  caches are cleaned up even when workers restart.
745	
746	Cache is an optimization only. Every Stage 2 turn must rebuild its context from
747	the database transcript, evidence store, issue state, and settings. A cache miss
748	may cost more tokens, but must not change behavior.
749	
750	### `connection: close` on all NAS API HTTP calls
751	
752	Undici's keep-alive pool exhausts after ~10–15 calls when timed-out requests do
753	not return their socket. NAS API is reached over Tailscale (sub-ms RTT), making
754	re-handshake cost negligible.
755	
756	### Executor kills the process group
757	
758	`executor.go` uses `cmd.SysProcAttr{Setpgid: true}` and
759	`syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` on timeout. `exec.CommandContext` kills only the
760	direct bash child; without process-group kill, `grep ... | head` orphans the
761	`grep` subprocess. Combined with the hard-block on recursive grep against Synology
762	internal stores, this prevents the runaway that ran for 4d11h on production.
763	
764	### `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_*` baked at build time
765	
766	These variables are passed as Docker build args in `web-image.yml` and embedded
767	into the Next.js client bundle at `docker build` time. Setting them in Coolify
768	after the image is built has no effect. A new push to `main` is required to change
769	them.
770	
771	---
772	
773	## Known constraints and blind spots
774	
775	- `container_status` CPU/mem always reads 0 — use `container_io` instead
776	- `scheduled_tasks` returns DSM error 103 on edgesynology1 (unsupported API version)
777	- Some snapshot-replication APIs are unsupported on certain DSM versions
778	- Log-derived fields are regex-parsed — categorizations imperfect, raw text faithful
779	- `analyzeRecentLogs` in `log-analyzer.ts` has no callers; tables preserved for a
780	  future AI clustering layer
781	- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not
782	  wired to any pipeline stage
783	- `drive_team_folders` is written by the agent but never queried by the web app
784	- `drive_team_folders_partitioned` has no child partitions and receives no writes;
785	  it is forward infrastructure — do not drop it
786	- `issue_resolutions` / `resolution_steps` / `resolution_log` / `resolution_messages`
787	  are superseded by the `issues` pipeline but not yet dropped
788	
789	---
790	
791	## Relay (`apps/relay`)
792	
793	A narrow named-action HTTP proxy for an external (Lovable) frontend. No CI
794	workflow exists — there is no `.github/workflows/relay-*.yml`. The relay image is
795	built and deployed manually on the VPS. Treat this as an exceptional deploy path,
796	not the routine one. See `apps/relay/OPERATIONS.md`.
797
