content.text:
This is the key structural insight: capabilities aren't bespoke code — they're **tool definitions in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`** that emit shell commands classified by **`apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`** and enabled in **`apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`**. And there's already a near-exact precedent: `set_vm_overcommit_memory` + `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` do `sysctl -w` plus a write to `/etc/sysctl.conf`.

Let me read the build/test doc and pull only the nas-api/validator-relevant parts of AGENTS.md (skipping the pipeline/web/db material that doesn't apply):

content.content:
1	# Development
2	
3	## Prerequisites
4	
5	- **Go 1.23+** with CGO enabled (agent requires `go-sqlite3`; nas-api does not but uses the same toolchain)
6	- **Node.js 22+** and **pnpm 9** (web app and nas-mcp)
7	- **Docker** (to run Supabase locally or build images)
8	- `gcc` and `musl-dev` (or equivalent libc headers) for CGO builds on Linux/Alpine
9	- Tailscale connected (for live NAS API calls from dev; not needed if using stored evidence only)
10	- Supabase CLI (`npm i -g supabase`) for local migration management
11	
12	## Local setup
13	
14	```sh
15	# From repo root
16	pnpm install          # install all workspace deps
17	pnpm build            # build all packages in dependency order (turbo)
18	                      # pipeline: shared:build → web:build, nas-mcp:build
19	```
20	
21	For the web app, copy the example env file and fill in values:
22	
23	```sh
24	cp apps/web/.env.example apps/web/.env.local
25	# Edit .env.local — at minimum set:
26	#   NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
27	#   SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY
28	#   NAS_EDGE1_API_URL, NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET, NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY
29	#   NAS_EDGE2_API_URL, NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET, NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY
30	#   OPENAI_API_KEY or ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
31	```
32	
33	`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are baked into the
34	Next.js client bundle at build time. In development `pnpm dev` reads them from
35	`.env.local` at runtime; in production they must be GitHub Secrets for the build
36	workflow to bake them in — changing them in Coolify after the image is built has no
37	effect.
38	
39	## Running each service locally
40	
41	### Web app (Next.js)
42	
43	```sh
44	cd apps/web
45	pnpm dev              # http://localhost:3000
46	```
47	
48	Or from the repo root:
49	
50	```sh
51	pnpm dev              # turbo dev — runs all workspace apps with a dev script
52	```
53	
54	The dashboard requires a live Supabase project (the production one is fine for
55	read-heavy dev). The issue agent requires `NAS_EDGE*` env vars and Tailscale
56	connectivity to reach the NAS APIs.
57	
58	### Agent (Go)
59	
60	```sh
61	cd apps/agent
62	
63	export DSM_URL=https://192.168.1.x:5001
64	export DSM_USERNAME=admin
65	export DSM_PASSWORD=...
66	export SUPABASE_URL=https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co
67	export SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY=...
68	export NAS_ID=4f1d7e2a-7d5d-4d5f-8b55-0f8efb0d1001   # must match nas_units.id
69	export NAS_NAME="Dev NAS"
70	
71	CGO_ENABLED=1 go run ./cmd/agent
72	```
73	
74	The agent exits immediately if `DSM_USERNAME`, `DSM_PASSWORD`, `SUPABASE_URL`,
75	`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`, or a UUID-format `NAS_ID` are missing. It will not start
76	with a placeholder `NAS_ID`.
77	
78	### NAS API (Go)
79	
80	```sh
81	cd apps/nas-api
82	
83	export NAS_API_SECRET=devSecret
84	export NAS_API_APPROVAL_SIGNING_KEY=devSigningKey
85	export DSM_USERNAME=admin
86	export DSM_PASSWORD=...
87	
88	CGO_ENABLED=0 go run ./cmd/server
89	# Listens on :7734
90	```
91	
92	Test that it started:
93	```sh
94	curl http://localhost:7734/health
95	# → {"status":"ok","build_sha":"dev","build_time":"unknown"}
96	```
97	
98	### NAS MCP server (Node.js + FastMCP)
99	
100	```sh
101	cd apps/nas-mcp
102	
103	export MCP_BEARER_TOKEN=devToken
104	export NAS_EDGE1_API_URL=http://100.107.131.35:7734
105	export NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET=...
106	export NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY=...
107	export NAS_EDGE2_API_URL=http://100.107.131.36:7734
108	export NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET=...
109	export NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY=...
110	
111	pnpm build            # compile TypeScript to dist/
112	pnpm start            # runs dist/index.js on port 3001
113	```
114	
115	The MCP server uses TypeScript FastMCP with stateless HTTP Stream transport. Do
116	not add persistent MCP session state. The always-on tool list should stay small:
117	`list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool`,
118	`run_command`, `check_disk_space`, and `restart_nas_api`. Add registry
119	capabilities in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` and let catalog/search/detail
120	describe them clearly instead of registering all tools eagerly.
121	
122	## Running tests
123	
124	### Go (agent + nas-api)
125	
126	```sh
127	# Agent
128	cd apps/agent && go test ./...
129	
130	# NAS API
131	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./...
132	```
133	
134	The validator tests are the most important — run them whenever you add or reclassify
135	a command pattern:
136	
137	```sh
138	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
139	```
140	
141	The NAS API validator uses Go's `regexp` package, which is RE2 syntax. Do not use
142	PCRE/JavaScript-only features such as lookahead, lookbehind, or backreferences in
143	`regexp.MustCompile` patterns. An invalid `MustCompile` pattern panics during
144	package initialization and crash-loops `synology-monitor-nas-api` after Watchtower
145	pulls the image. If the local machine lacks Go, run the validator tests in Docker:
146	
147	```sh
148	docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
149	  go test ./internal/validator/...
150	```
151	
152	### TypeScript
153	
154	There are no Jest/Vitest test suites in the TS workspace. The CI equivalent is:
155	
156	```sh
157	pnpm type-check       # TypeScript check across all apps (from repo root)
158	```
159	
160	The web image build also runs an AI cache guard before Docker build:
161	
162	```sh
163	pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai
164	```
165	
166	This guard is a hard build blocker in CI (`web-image.yml`). Run it locally after
167	touching pipeline prompt ordering, provider usage fields, or context compiler logic.
168	
169	## Linting
170	
171	```sh
172	pnpm lint             # ESLint across all apps (from repo root)
173	pnpm type-check       # TypeScript type check across all apps
174	```
175	
176	Per-app:
177	
178	```sh
179	cd apps/web && pnpm lint
180	cd apps/nas-mcp && pnpm lint
181	```
182	
183	There is no Go linter wired into the monorepo scripts. Run `go vet ./...` manually
184	in `apps/agent` or `apps/nas-api` if needed.
185	
186	## Key source areas
187	
188	```
189	apps/web/src/
190	  app/
191	    (dashboard)/      — operator-facing pages (metrics, logs, sync-triage, settings, etc.)
192	    api/              — API routes
193	  lib/server/
194	    ai/               — 3-stage pipeline:
195	      stage1-structurer.ts   — deterministic dedup + evidence persistence
196	      stage2-reasoning.ts    — agentic reasoning loop with tool execution
197	      stage3-explainer.ts    — operator message + durable memory extraction
198	      pipeline-v2.ts         — orchestrator
199	      stage2-turn.ts         — turn state machine (terminals, approval persistence)
200	      fetch-evidence.ts      — fetch_evidence tool implementation
201	      context-compiler.ts    — stable→dynamic block ordering for cache correctness
202	      call-model.ts          — provider-agnostic model call wrapper
203	    issue-agent.ts     — telemetry gathering (gatherTelemetryContext)
204	    issue-detector.ts  — fingerprinting raw telemetry into issues
205	    issue-store.ts     — issue CRUD
206	    issue-workflow.ts  — job queue + status transitions
207	    nas-api-client.ts  — HTTP client for nas-api
208	    ai-settings.ts     — model selection + fallback chain
209	    copilot.ts         — legacy NAS assistant chat
210	  hooks/               — React hooks for dashboard data
211	
212	apps/agent/internal/
213	  collector/           — per-domain telemetry collectors (system, drive, diskstats, etc.)
214	  logwatcher/          — inotify + tail-based log watcher
215	  sender/              — SQLite WAL + Supabase flush, poison-row isolation
216	  security/            — inotify-driven security event watcher
217	
218	apps/nas-api/internal/
219	  validator/           — tier classification + hard-block list
220	  executor/            — subprocess runner (process group kill, WaitDelay)
221	  auth/                — bearer token + HMAC verifier
222	
223	packages/shared/src/
224	  nas-tools.ts         — 119 NAS tool definitions (McpToolDef, ALL_TOOL_DEFS)
225	```
226	
227	## Database migrations
228	
229	Migrations are in `supabase/migrations/`. Naming convention:
230	`000NN_short_description.sql` where NN is the next number after the current max.
231	
232	Current max: **00041**.
233	
234	To apply locally with the Supabase CLI:
235	
236	```sh
237	supabase db push      # apply pending migrations to your local Supabase
238	```
239	
240	Applied migrations are permanent. Do not edit them. Write a new migration to
241	correct a previous one.
242	
243	## Monorepo build pipeline
244	
245	```sh
246	pnpm install          # install all workspace deps
247	pnpm build            # build all packages (turbo)
248	pnpm type-check       # TypeScript check across all apps
249	pnpm lint             # lint across all apps
250	pnpm check:dashboard-data   # runs scripts/check-dashboard-data.mjs
251	```
252	
253	Turbo caches build outputs in `.turbo/`. The dependency order:
254	`shared:build → (web:build, nas-mcp:build)`.
255	
256	## Workflow: adding a new NAS tool
257	
258	This is the most common extension task. A "NAS tool" is a predefined diagnostic or
259	remediation command the MCP server and/or the Stage 2 issue agent can invoke.
260	
261	### Step 1 — Define the tool in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`
262	
263	Add an entry to `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`:
264	
265	```typescript
266	{
267	  name: "my_new_tool",
268	  description: "What it does — be specific, Stage 2 picks tools by description.",
269	  write: false,          // true if it modifies NAS state (tier 2/3)
270	  params: {
271	    target,              // import the shared target param
272	    // add any other zod params your command needs
273	    filter: z.string().optional().describe("optional search term"),
274	  },
275	  buildCommand: (input) => [
276	    "echo '=== MY TOOL ==='",
277	    `grep -r ${quote(String(input.filter ?? ""))} /var/log/my-log.log | tail -n 50`,
278	  ].join("\n"),
279	},
280	```
281	
282	Add it to `TOOL_GROUPS` in the same file so `tool_search` can find it by group:
283	
284	```typescript
285	const TOOL_GROUPS: Record<string, string> = {
286	  // ... existing entries ...
287	  my_new_tool: "logs",   // pick the closest group
288	};
289	```
290	
291	If you skip `TOOL_GROUPS`, the tool falls into `"misc"` and startup logs a warning.
292	It still works, but won't surface in group-based searches.
293	
294	Path rule for NAS tools: commands run inside the `synology-monitor-nas-api`
295	container. Do not assume the telemetry agent's mount layout. Package state is
296	mounted at `/host/packages`, DSM binaries at `/host/usr/syno`, DSM logs at
297	`/host/log`, and the full Btrfs volume at `/btrfs/volumeN`. Individual shared
298	folders are mounted under `/volume1/<share>`, but that is not enough for snapshot
299	enumeration. If DSM 7 hides scheduler or Snapshot Replication details behind
300	package state or WebAPI, prefer narrow read-only path discovery plus DSM WebAPI
301	`list`/`query` methods over broad filesystem scans.
302	
303	### Step 2 — Enable the tool in `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`
304	
305	Add the tool name to `enabled_read_tools` (read-only) or `enabled_write_tools`
306	(state-modifying):
307	
308	```json
309	{
310	  "enabled_read_tools": [
311	    "...",
312	    "my_new_tool"
313	  ]
314	}
315	```
316	
317	A tool present in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `tools-config.json` is invisible
318	to `tool_search` and rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. This lets
319	you ship a tool dark and enable it without a code deploy.
320	
321	### Step 3 — For write tools: update the NAS API validator
322	
323	If `write: true`, the validator at `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`
324	must be able to classify the generated command at the right tier.
325	
326	- **Tier 2** (service ops — reversible, no `/volume*` touch): the command probably
327	  already matches a `writePatterns` regex. Verify with a test.
328	- **Tier 3** (file ops — touches `/volume*`): ensure a `filePatterns` entry matches.
329	- **DSM-managed container lifecycle**: use DSM WebAPI (`SYNO.Docker.Container`)
330	  commands and add tight `allowedServiceCommands` entries/tests for the exact
331	  container name. Routine `docker compose` lifecycle commands are blocked because
332	  they can desync Synology Container Manager's GUI state.
333	
334	Always add a test in `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go`:
335	
336	```go
337	{name: "my_new_tool tier2", command: "synopkg restart MyPackage", wantTier: 2},
338	```
339	
340	Regex rule: `apps/nas-api` is Go, so `regexp.MustCompile` patterns must be valid
341	Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
342	exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
343	both the matching and exception cases.
344	
345	Run:
346	```sh
347	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
348	```
349	
350	If the local machine lacks Go, use the same containerized test command as CI/debug
351	sessions:
352	
353	```sh
354	docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
355	  go test ./internal/validator/...
356	```
357	
358	### Step 4 — Stage 2 picks it up automatically
359	
360	Stage 2's `buildStage2Tools()` in `stage2-reasoning.ts` loads all
361	`ALL_TOOL_DEFS.filter(def => !def.write)` automatically. No registration needed.
362	Write tools never appear in Stage 2 (the agent proposes remediations; it does not
363	auto-execute tier-2/3 actions).
364	
365	### Step 5 — Build and verify
366	
367	```sh
368	pnpm build            # from repo root — compiles shared, web, nas-mcp
369	pnpm type-check
370	```
371	
372	Push to `main`. `nas-mcp-image.yml` triggers on changes to `apps/nas-mcp/**` and
373	`packages/shared/**` and redeploys the MCP server. `web-image.yml` also watches
374	`packages/shared/**`, so shared tool changes may build/redeploy the web image too.
375	
376	## Workflow: adding a new metric or telemetry type
377	
378	This is the pipeline for getting a new data source into the issue agent's evidence
379	store and prompt.
380	
381	### Step 1 — Agent collector
382	
383	Create `apps/agent/internal/collector/yourname.go`. The collector must:
384	
385	1. Implement a `Run(stop <-chan struct{})` method.
386	2. Use the WaitGroup pattern when wired in `main.go`:
387	
388	   ```go
389	   wg.Add(1)
390	   go func() {
391	       defer wg.Done()
392	       yourCollector.Run(stop)
393	   }()
394	   ```
395	
396	   Omitting `wg.Add(1)` means graceful shutdown returns before the collector
397	   finishes, silently dropping in-flight WAL writes. This exact bug existed in the
398	   ShareSync collector (see AGENTS.md §10).
399	
400	3. Call `sender.Queue*` to buffer rows. Add a payload type to `sender/types.go`
401	   and a `Queue*` method to `sender/sender.go` if this is a new table.
402	
403	4. To persist a cursor between restarts, use `sender.SaveCheckpoint` /
404	   `sender.LoadCheckpoint` (writes to the `checkpoints` SQLite table in the WAL).
405	
406	5. Access host kernel data at `/host/proc/...` and `/host/sys/...` — the NAS
407	   compose mounts `/proc:/host/proc:ro` and `/sys:/host/sys:ro`. See `diskstats.go`
408	   and `sysextras.go` for examples.
409	
410	**Do not add a sender payload field without a matching Supabase column.** The
411	PostgREST batch will reject the row and trigger poison-row isolation (5 retries,
412	then drop).
413	
414	### Step 2 — Database migration
415	
416	Add a migration for the new table or column:
417	
418	```sh
419	# Next number after 00041:
420	supabase/migrations/00042_add_my_new_table.sql
421	```
422	
423	For a new time-series table, follow the pattern in existing migrations for
424	pg_partman monthly partitioning. Applied migrations are immutable — never edit
425	them.
426	
427	### Step 3 — Supabase sender
428	
429	If the new data belongs in an existing table (e.g. a new `type` in `metrics`), no
430	schema change is needed. If it is a new table:
431	
432	- Add to `upsertTables` in `sender/sender.go` **only** if the table uses
433	  merge-on-conflict semantics (like `package_status`). All other tables are
434	  append-only inserts.
435	- Add the table name to the Go sender payload types in `sender/types.go`.
436	
437	### Step 4 — `gatherTelemetryContext` in `issue-agent.ts`
438	
439	Add a Supabase query for the new table in `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-agent.ts`
440	inside the `Promise.all([...])` block:
441	
442	```typescript
443	supabase
444	  .from("my_new_table")
445	  .select("nas_id, captured_at, my_field, another_field")
446	  .gte("captured_at", since6h)
447	  .order("captured_at", { ascending: false })
448	  .limit(20),
449	```
450	
451	Collect the result:
452	
453	```typescript
454	const myNewData = collectResult("my_new_data", myNewResult, telemetry_errors);
455	```
456	
457	Return it from the function:
458	
459	```typescript
460	return {
461	  // ... existing fields ...
462	  my_new_data: myNewData,
463	};
464	```
465	
466	### Step 5 — Stage 1 `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` in `stage1-structurer.ts`
467	
468	Add a `SourceMap` entry to the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` array in
469	`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage1-structurer.ts`:
470	
471	```typescript
472	{
473	  key: "my_new_data",         // must match the key returned by gatherTelemetryContext
474	  source: "my_source_label",  // appears in evidence bodies as "source/severity: body"
475	  tsFields: ["captured_at"],  // fields tried in order for the timestamp
476	  // bodyFields: ["field1", "field2"], // optional: joined with " — "; omit to use full JSON
477	},
478	```
479	
480	**Omit `bodyFields` when diagnostic numbers must be visible.** If you use
481	`bodyFields`, only those fields form the evidence body — metric values land in
482	`metadata` which `fetch_evidence` never returns, making the data invisible to Stage
483	2. See the comment on `top_processes` in the source file.
484	
485	Stage 1 runs automatically before every Stage 2 turn. Once added here, the new data
486	surfaces in `issue_evidence_items` and the bounded evidence slice that Stage 2
487	receives in its prompt.
488	
489	### Step 6 — Stage 2 taxonomy (optional)
490	
491	If the new data requires interpretation rules (e.g. thresholds, DSM blind spots,
492	known false-positive patterns), add them to the `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant in
493	`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`. Keep additions concise — the
494	taxonomy is in the stable cacheable prefix, so additions persist across all turns.
495	
496	### Step 7 — Verify the pipeline
497	
498	```sh
499	pnpm type-check       # catch type mismatches across issue-agent / stage1 / stage2
500	```
501	
502	Check that a new issue run populates `issue_evidence_items` with the expected
503	`source` label. The Supabase dashboard or `fetch_evidence` (from Stage 2) can
504	confirm rows are landing.
505	
506	## Debugging
507	
508	### Agent not sending data
509	
510	1. Check `/app/data/wal.db` size — if growing, data is queuing but not flushing.
511	2. Check agent container logs: `docker logs synology-monitor-agent`.
512	3. Check `nas_logs` and `metrics` freshness in Supabase. If metrics are fresh but
513	   logs are stale, one source is hitting a bad row — check the sender log for
514	   `[sender] isolating bad row` lines.
515	4. An empty table is a bug, not a healthy state. A collector may be hitting an
516	   unsupported DSM API — check `nas_logs` for `API unavailable` or error 103 entries.
517	
518	### NAS API not responding
519	
520	1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS.
521	2. Verify Tailscale is connected: the web app reaches NAS API over Tailscale IPs
522	   (`100.107.131.35:7734` / `100.107.131.36:7734`).
523	3. Health check: `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}`.
524	   This endpoint requires no auth.
525	4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
526	   not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
527	5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.
528	   Check `docker ps -a` and `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100`. If
529	   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
530	   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.
531	
532	### Stage 2 / issue-agent not running
533	
534	1. Check `issue_jobs` table — are jobs being enqueued with `status='pending'`?
535	2. Check `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` env var:
536	   - `inline` — jobs drain on the next API request to the dashboard
537	   - `background` — a separate worker loop drains via `ISSUE_WORKER_TOKEN`
538	3. Check `ai_model_calls` table for model errors (rate limits, invalid key, context
539	   window exceeded).
540	4. Check `issue_stage_runs` for per-stage failure messages.
541	
542	### Stage 1 evidence missing
543	
544	If Stage 2 says evidence is empty but data is in the DB:
545	
546	1. Check `issue_evidence_items` for the issue ID — Stage 1 replaces rows on each
547	   run (idempotent delete + insert).
548	2. Check the `source` label in `issue_evidence_items` against `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`
549	   in `stage1-structurer.ts` — a mismatch in the `key` field silently skips the
550	   source.
551	3. Check `gatherTelemetryContext` return value — the key must match exactly.
552	
553	### NAS API validator rejecting a command
554	
555	Run the validator tests with the new command to diagnose the tier:
556	
557	```sh
558	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/... -v
559	```
560	
561	`ClassifyTier` returns `-1` for hard-blocked, `1` for read-only, `2` for service
562	ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
563	matches.
564	
565	If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
566	pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
567	will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.
568	
569	### Type errors
570	
571	```sh
572	pnpm type-check
573	```
574	
575	The most common causes:
576	
577	- A new DB column added without a matching TypeScript type annotation.
578	- A `fetch()` call without proper response typing.
579	- A new `gatherTelemetryContext` return field not reflected in `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`.
580	- The `shared` package not rebuilt after changing `nas-tools.ts` — run `pnpm build`
581	  from the repo root to regenerate `packages/shared/dist/`.
582	
583	### Build: `guard:ai` CI check failing
584	
585	The web app has an AI cache guard (`pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai`)
586	that enforces stable→dynamic prompt block ordering and normalized provider usage
587	fields. If the CI build fails at this step, run the guard locally:
588	
589	```sh
590	cd apps/web && pnpm run guard:ai
591	```
592	
593	The error message identifies which file and which invariant failed.
594	
595	## WAL mechanism
596	
597	If the agent cannot reach Supabase (network outage, maintenance), all telemetry
598	queues in `/app/data/wal.db` on the NAS disk. The WAL is capped at
599	`MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB) — oldest entries are dropped at the cap. Once
600	connectivity resumes, the agent flushes the backlog automatically.
601	
602	The `checkpoints` table in the same SQLite file stores durable collector cursors
603	(log file byte offsets, ShareSync watermarks, etc.) so collectors resume from the
604	right position after restarts.
605	
606	**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx`, the sender re-sends each row in
607	the failed batch individually. Good rows land; only the bad row accumulates retries
608	(max 5, then dropped). This was added after a single bad row silently froze log and
609	alert ingestion for 19h and 23 days respectively (see AGENTS.md §13).
610	
611	## Custom metric schedules
612	
613	To schedule a recurring shell command without a code change or deploy:
614	
615	```sql
616	INSERT INTO custom_metric_schedules
617	  (name, description, nas_id, collection_command, interval_minutes, is_active, next_run_at)
618	VALUES
619	  ('my_check', 'What it does', 'edgesynology1', 'cat /proc/loadavg', 60, true, now());
620	```
621	
622	The agent polls every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and stores
623	output in `custom_metric_data`. Stage 2 can read recent output via `fetch_evidence`
624	(source: `custom_metric_data` rows surface after the next Stage 1 run for the issue).
625	
626	Available paths in the agent container:
627	
628	| Host path | Container path |
629	|---|---|
630	| `/proc` | `/host/proc` |
631	| `/sys` | `/host/sys` |
632	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` |
633	| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` |
634	| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` |
635	| (other shares) | `/host/shares/<name>` |
636

file.content:
# Development

## Prerequisites

- **Go 1.23+** with CGO enabled (agent requires `go-sqlite3`; nas-api does not but uses the same toolchain)
- **Node.js 22+** and **pnpm 9** (web app and nas-mcp)
- **Docker** (to run Supabase locally or build images)
- `gcc` and `musl-dev` (or equivalent libc headers) for CGO builds on Linux/Alpine
- Tailscale connected (for live NAS API calls from dev; not needed if using stored evidence only)
- Supabase CLI (`npm i -g supabase`) for local migration management

## Local setup

```sh
# From repo root
pnpm install          # install all workspace deps
pnpm build            # build all packages in dependency order (turbo)
                      # pipeline: shared:build → web:build, nas-mcp:build
```

For the web app, copy the example env file and fill in values:

```sh
cp apps/web/.env.example apps/web/.env.local
# Edit .env.local — at minimum set:
#   NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
#   SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY
#   NAS_EDGE1_API_URL, NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET, NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY
#   NAS_EDGE2_API_URL, NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET, NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY
#   OPENAI_API_KEY or ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
```

`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are baked into the
Next.js client bundle at build time. In development `pnpm dev` reads them from
`.env.local` at runtime; in production they must be GitHub Secrets for the build
workflow to bake them in — changing them in Coolify after the image is built has no
effect.

## Running each service locally

### Web app (Next.js)

```sh
cd apps/web
pnpm dev              # http://localhost:3000
```

Or from the repo root:

```sh
pnpm dev              # turbo dev — runs all workspace apps with a dev script
```

The dashboard requires a live Supabase project (the production one is fine for
read-heavy dev). The issue agent requires `NAS_EDGE*` env vars and Tailscale
connectivity to reach the NAS APIs.

### Agent (Go)

```sh
cd apps/agent

export DSM_URL=https://192.168.1.x:5001
export DSM_USERNAME=admin
export DSM_PASSWORD=...
export SUPABASE_URL=https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co
export SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY=...
export NAS_ID=4f1d7e2a-7d5d-4d5f-8b55-0f8efb0d1001   # must match nas_units.id
export NAS_NAME="Dev NAS"

CGO_ENABLED=1 go run ./cmd/agent
```

The agent exits immediately if `DSM_USERNAME`, `DSM_PASSWORD`, `SUPABASE_URL`,
`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`, or a UUID-format `NAS_ID` are missing. It will not start
with a placeholder `NAS_ID`.

### NAS API (Go)

```sh
cd apps/nas-api

export NAS_API_SECRET=devSecret
export NAS_API_APPROVAL_SIGNING_KEY=devSigningKey
export DSM_USERNAME=admin
export DSM_PASSWORD=...

CGO_ENABLED=0 go run ./cmd/server
# Listens on :7734
```

Test that it started:
```sh
curl http://localhost:7734/health
# → {"status":"ok","build_sha":"dev","build_time":"unknown"}
```

### NAS MCP server (Node.js + FastMCP)

```sh
cd apps/nas-mcp

export MCP_BEARER_TOKEN=devToken
export NAS_EDGE1_API_URL=http://100.107.131.35:7734
export NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET=...
export NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY=...
export NAS_EDGE2_API_URL=http://100.107.131.36:7734
export NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET=...
export NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY=...

pnpm build            # compile TypeScript to dist/
pnpm start            # runs dist/index.js on port 3001
```

The MCP server uses TypeScript FastMCP with stateless HTTP Stream transport. Do
not add persistent MCP session state. The always-on tool list should stay small:
`list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool`,
`run_command`, `check_disk_space`, and `restart_nas_api`. Add registry
capabilities in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` and let catalog/search/detail
describe them clearly instead of registering all tools eagerly.

## Running tests

### Go (agent + nas-api)

```sh
# Agent
cd apps/agent && go test ./...

# NAS API
cd apps/nas-api && go test ./...
```

The validator tests are the most important — run them whenever you add or reclassify
a command pattern:

```sh
cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
```

The NAS API validator uses Go's `regexp` package, which is RE2 syntax. Do not use
PCRE/JavaScript-only features such as lookahead, lookbehind, or backreferences in
`regexp.MustCompile` patterns. An invalid `MustCompile` pattern panics during
package initialization and crash-loops `synology-monitor-nas-api` after Watchtower
pulls the image. If the local machine lacks Go, run the validator tests in Docker:

```sh
docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
  go test ./internal/validator/...
```

### TypeScript

There are no Jest/Vitest test suites in the TS workspace. The CI equivalent is:

```sh
pnpm type-check       # TypeScript check across all apps (from repo root)
```

The web image build also runs an AI cache guard before Docker build:

```sh
pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai
```

This guard is a hard build blocker in CI (`web-image.yml`). Run it locally after
touching pipeline prompt ordering, provider usage fields, or context compiler logic.

## Linting

```sh
pnpm lint             # ESLint across all apps (from repo root)
pnpm type-check       # TypeScript type check across all apps
```

Per-app:

```sh
cd apps/web && pnpm lint
cd apps/nas-mcp && pnpm lint
```

There is no Go linter wired into the monorepo scripts. Run `go vet ./...` manually
in `apps/agent` or `apps/nas-api` if needed.

## Key source areas

```
apps/web/src/
  app/
    (dashboard)/      — operator-facing pages (metrics, logs, sync-triage, settings, etc.)
    api/              — API routes
  lib/server/
    ai/               — 3-stage pipeline:
      stage1-structurer.ts   — deterministic dedup + evidence persistence
      stage2-reasoning.ts    — agentic reasoning loop with tool execution
      stage3-explainer.ts    — operator message + durable memory extraction
      pipeline-v2.ts         — orchestrator
      stage2-turn.ts         — turn state machine (terminals, approval persistence)
      fetch-evidence.ts      — fetch_evidence tool implementation
      context-compiler.ts    — stable→dynamic block ordering for cache correctness
      call-model.ts          — provider-agnostic model call wrapper
    issue-agent.ts     — telemetry gathering (gatherTelemetryContext)
    issue-detector.ts  — fingerprinting raw telemetry into issues
    issue-store.ts     — issue CRUD
    issue-workflow.ts  — job queue + status transitions
    nas-api-client.ts  — HTTP client for nas-api
    ai-settings.ts     — model selection + fallback chain
    copilot.ts         — legacy NAS assistant chat
  hooks/               — React hooks for dashboard data

apps/agent/internal/
  collector/           — per-domain telemetry collectors (system, drive, diskstats, etc.)
  logwatcher/          — inotify + tail-based log watcher
  sender/              — SQLite WAL + Supabase flush, poison-row isolation
  security/            — inotify-driven security event watcher

apps/nas-api/internal/
  validator/           — tier classification + hard-block list
  executor/            — subprocess runner (process group kill, WaitDelay)
  auth/                — bearer token + HMAC verifier

packages/shared/src/
  nas-tools.ts         — 119 NAS tool definitions (McpToolDef, ALL_TOOL_DEFS)
```

## Database migrations

Migrations are in `supabase/migrations/`. Naming convention:
`000NN_short_description.sql` where NN is the next number after the current max.

Current max: **00041**.

To apply locally with the Supabase CLI:

```sh
supabase db push      # apply pending migrations to your local Supabase
```

Applied migrations are permanent. Do not edit them. Write a new migration to
correct a previous one.

## Monorepo build pipeline

```sh
pnpm install          # install all workspace deps
pnpm build            # build all packages (turbo)
pnpm type-check       # TypeScript check across all apps
pnpm lint             # lint across all apps
pnpm check:dashboard-data   # runs scripts/check-dashboard-data.mjs
```

Turbo caches build outputs in `.turbo/`. The dependency order:
`shared:build → (web:build, nas-mcp:build)`.

## Workflow: adding a new NAS tool

This is the most common extension task. A "NAS tool" is a predefined diagnostic or
remediation command the MCP server and/or the Stage 2 issue agent can invoke.

### Step 1 — Define the tool in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`

Add an entry to `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`:

```typescript
{
  name: "my_new_tool",
  description: "What it does — be specific, Stage 2 picks tools by description.",
  write: false,          // true if it modifies NAS state (tier 2/3)
  params: {
    target,              // import the shared target param
    // add any other zod params your command needs
    filter: z.string().optional().describe("optional search term"),
  },
  buildCommand: (input) => [
    "echo '=== MY TOOL ==='",
    `grep -r ${quote(String(input.filter ?? ""))} /var/log/my-log.log | tail -n 50`,
  ].join("\n"),
},
```

Add it to `TOOL_GROUPS` in the same file so `tool_search` can find it by group:

```typescript
const TOOL_GROUPS: Record<string, string> = {
  // ... existing entries ...
  my_new_tool: "logs",   // pick the closest group
};
```

If you skip `TOOL_GROUPS`, the tool falls into `"misc"` and startup logs a warning.
It still works, but won't surface in group-based searches.

Path rule for NAS tools: commands run inside the `synology-monitor-nas-api`
container. Do not assume the telemetry agent's mount layout. Package state is
mounted at `/host/packages`, DSM binaries at `/host/usr/syno`, DSM logs at
`/host/log`, and the full Btrfs volume at `/btrfs/volumeN`. Individual shared
folders are mounted under `/volume1/<share>`, but that is not enough for snapshot
enumeration. If DSM 7 hides scheduler or Snapshot Replication details behind
package state or WebAPI, prefer narrow read-only path discovery plus DSM WebAPI
`list`/`query` methods over broad filesystem scans.

### Step 2 — Enable the tool in `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`

Add the tool name to `enabled_read_tools` (read-only) or `enabled_write_tools`
(state-modifying):

```json
{
  "enabled_read_tools": [
    "...",
    "my_new_tool"
  ]
}
```

A tool present in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `tools-config.json` is invisible
to `tool_search` and rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. This lets
you ship a tool dark and enable it without a code deploy.

### Step 3 — For write tools: update the NAS API validator

If `write: true`, the validator at `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`
must be able to classify the generated command at the right tier.

- **Tier 2** (service ops — reversible, no `/volume*` touch): the command probably
  already matches a `writePatterns` regex. Verify with a test.
- **Tier 3** (file ops — touches `/volume*`): ensure a `filePatterns` entry matches.
- **DSM-managed container lifecycle**: use DSM WebAPI (`SYNO.Docker.Container`)
  commands and add tight `allowedServiceCommands` entries/tests for the exact
  container name. Routine `docker compose` lifecycle commands are blocked because
  they can desync Synology Container Manager's GUI state.

Always add a test in `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go`:

```go
{name: "my_new_tool tier2", command: "synopkg restart MyPackage", wantTier: 2},
```

Regex rule: `apps/nas-api` is Go, so `regexp.MustCompile` patterns must be valid
Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
both the matching and exception cases.

Run:
```sh
cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
```

If the local machine lacks Go, use the same containerized test command as CI/debug
sessions:

```sh
docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
  go test ./internal/validator/...
```

### Step 4 — Stage 2 picks it up automatically

Stage 2's `buildStage2Tools()` in `stage2-reasoning.ts` loads all
`ALL_TOOL_DEFS.filter(def => !def.write)` automatically. No registration needed.
Write tools never appear in Stage 2 (the agent proposes remediations; it does not
auto-execute tier-2/3 actions).

### Step 5 — Build and verify

```sh
pnpm build            # from repo root — compiles shared, web, nas-mcp
pnpm type-check
```

Push to `main`. `nas-mcp-image.yml` triggers on changes to `apps/nas-mcp/**` and
`packages/shared/**` and redeploys the MCP server. `web-image.yml` also watches
`packages/shared/**`, so shared tool changes may build/redeploy the web image too.

## Workflow: adding a new metric or telemetry type

This is the pipeline for getting a new data source into the issue agent's evidence
store and prompt.

### Step 1 — Agent collector

Create `apps/agent/internal/collector/yourname.go`. The collector must:

1. Implement a `Run(stop <-chan struct{})` method.
2. Use the WaitGroup pattern when wired in `main.go`:

   ```go
   wg.Add(1)
   go func() {
       defer wg.Done()
       yourCollector.Run(stop)
   }()
   ```

   Omitting `wg.Add(1)` means graceful shutdown returns before the collector
   finishes, silently dropping in-flight WAL writes. This exact bug existed in the
   ShareSync collector (see AGENTS.md §10).

3. Call `sender.Queue*` to buffer rows. Add a payload type to `sender/types.go`
   and a `Queue*` method to `sender/sender.go` if this is a new table.

4. To persist a cursor between restarts, use `sender.SaveCheckpoint` /
   `sender.LoadCheckpoint` (writes to the `checkpoints` SQLite table in the WAL).

5. Access host kernel data at `/host/proc/...` and `/host/sys/...` — the NAS
   compose mounts `/proc:/host/proc:ro` and `/sys:/host/sys:ro`. See `diskstats.go`
   and `sysextras.go` for examples.

**Do not add a sender payload field without a matching Supabase column.** The
PostgREST batch will reject the row and trigger poison-row isolation (5 retries,
then drop).

### Step 2 — Database migration

Add a migration for the new table or column:

```sh
# Next number after 00041:
supabase/migrations/00042_add_my_new_table.sql
```

For a new time-series table, follow the pattern in existing migrations for
pg_partman monthly partitioning. Applied migrations are immutable — never edit
them.

### Step 3 — Supabase sender

If the new data belongs in an existing table (e.g. a new `type` in `metrics`), no
schema change is needed. If it is a new table:

- Add to `upsertTables` in `sender/sender.go` **only** if the table uses
  merge-on-conflict semantics (like `package_status`). All other tables are
  append-only inserts.
- Add the table name to the Go sender payload types in `sender/types.go`.

### Step 4 — `gatherTelemetryContext` in `issue-agent.ts`

Add a Supabase query for the new table in `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-agent.ts`
inside the `Promise.all([...])` block:

```typescript
supabase
  .from("my_new_table")
  .select("nas_id, captured_at, my_field, another_field")
  .gte("captured_at", since6h)
  .order("captured_at", { ascending: false })
  .limit(20),
```

Collect the result:

```typescript
const myNewData = collectResult("my_new_data", myNewResult, telemetry_errors);
```

Return it from the function:

```typescript
return {
  // ... existing fields ...
  my_new_data: myNewData,
};
```

### Step 5 — Stage 1 `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` in `stage1-structurer.ts`

Add a `SourceMap` entry to the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` array in
`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage1-structurer.ts`:

```typescript
{
  key: "my_new_data",         // must match the key returned by gatherTelemetryContext
  source: "my_source_label",  // appears in evidence bodies as "source/severity: body"
  tsFields: ["captured_at"],  // fields tried in order for the timestamp
  // bodyFields: ["field1", "field2"], // optional: joined with " — "; omit to use full JSON
},
```

**Omit `bodyFields` when diagnostic numbers must be visible.** If you use
`bodyFields`, only those fields form the evidence body — metric values land in
`metadata` which `fetch_evidence` never returns, making the data invisible to Stage
2. See the comment on `top_processes` in the source file.

Stage 1 runs automatically before every Stage 2 turn. Once added here, the new data
surfaces in `issue_evidence_items` and the bounded evidence slice that Stage 2
receives in its prompt.

### Step 6 — Stage 2 taxonomy (optional)

If the new data requires interpretation rules (e.g. thresholds, DSM blind spots,
known false-positive patterns), add them to the `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant in
`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`. Keep additions concise — the
taxonomy is in the stable cacheable prefix, so additions persist across all turns.

### Step 7 — Verify the pipeline

```sh
pnpm type-check       # catch type mismatches across issue-agent / stage1 / stage2
```

Check that a new issue run populates `issue_evidence_items` with the expected
`source` label. The Supabase dashboard or `fetch_evidence` (from Stage 2) can
confirm rows are landing.

## Debugging

### Agent not sending data

1. Check `/app/data/wal.db` size — if growing, data is queuing but not flushing.
2. Check agent container logs: `docker logs synology-monitor-agent`.
3. Check `nas_logs` and `metrics` freshness in Supabase. If metrics are fresh but
   logs are stale, one source is hitting a bad row — check the sender log for
   `[sender] isolating bad row` lines.
4. An empty table is a bug, not a healthy state. A collector may be hitting an
   unsupported DSM API — check `nas_logs` for `API unavailable` or error 103 entries.

### NAS API not responding

1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS.
2. Verify Tailscale is connected: the web app reaches NAS API over Tailscale IPs
   (`100.107.131.35:7734` / `100.107.131.36:7734`).
3. Health check: `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}`.
   This endpoint requires no auth.
4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
   not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.
   Check `docker ps -a` and `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100`. If
   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.

### Stage 2 / issue-agent not running

1. Check `issue_jobs` table — are jobs being enqueued with `status='pending'`?
2. Check `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` env var:
   - `inline` — jobs drain on the next API request to the dashboard
   - `background` — a separate worker loop drains via `ISSUE_WORKER_TOKEN`
3. Check `ai_model_calls` table for model errors (rate limits, invalid key, context
   window exceeded).
4. Check `issue_stage_runs` for per-stage failure messages.

### Stage 1 evidence missing

If Stage 2 says evidence is empty but data is in the DB:

1. Check `issue_evidence_items` for the issue ID — Stage 1 replaces rows on each
   run (idempotent delete + insert).
2. Check the `source` label in `issue_evidence_items` against `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`
   in `stage1-structurer.ts` — a mismatch in the `key` field silently skips the
   source.
3. Check `gatherTelemetryContext` return value — the key must match exactly.

### NAS API validator rejecting a command

Run the validator tests with the new command to diagnose the tier:

```sh
cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/... -v
```

`ClassifyTier` returns `-1` for hard-blocked, `1` for read-only, `2` for service
ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
matches.

If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.

### Type errors

```sh
pnpm type-check
```

The most common causes:

- A new DB column added without a matching TypeScript type annotation.
- A `fetch()` call without proper response typing.
- A new `gatherTelemetryContext` return field not reflected in `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`.
- The `shared` package not rebuilt after changing `nas-tools.ts` — run `pnpm build`
  from the repo root to regenerate `packages/shared/dist/`.

### Build: `guard:ai` CI check failing

The web app has an AI cache guard (`pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai`)
that enforces stable→dynamic prompt block ordering and normalized provider usage
fields. If the CI build fails at this step, run the guard locally:

```sh
cd apps/web && pnpm run guard:ai
```

The error message identifies which file and which invariant failed.

## WAL mechanism

If the agent cannot reach Supabase (network outage, maintenance), all telemetry
queues in `/app/data/wal.db` on the NAS disk. The WAL is capped at
`MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB) — oldest entries are dropped at the cap. Once
connectivity resumes, the agent flushes the backlog automatically.

The `checkpoints` table in the same SQLite file stores durable collector cursors
(log file byte offsets, ShareSync watermarks, etc.) so collectors resume from the
right position after restarts.

**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx`, the sender re-sends each row in
the failed batch individually. Good rows land; only the bad row accumulates retries
(max 5, then dropped). This was added after a single bad row silently froze log and
alert ingestion for 19h and 23 days respectively (see AGENTS.md §13).

## Custom metric schedules

To schedule a recurring shell command without a code change or deploy:

```sql
INSERT INTO custom_metric_schedules
  (name, description, nas_id, collection_command, interval_minutes, is_active, next_run_at)
VALUES
  ('my_check', 'What it does', 'edgesynology1', 'cat /proc/loadavg', 60, true, now());
```

The agent polls every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and stores
output in `custom_metric_data`. Stage 2 can read recent output via `fetch_evidence`
(source: `custom_metric_data` rows surface after the next Stage 1 run for the issue).

Available paths in the agent container:

| Host path | Container path |
|---|---|
| `/proc` | `/host/proc` |
| `/sys` | `/host/sys` |
| `/var/log` | `/host/log` |
| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` |
| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` |
| (other shares) | `/host/shares/<name>` |

content.text: Tier is decided by nas-api `/preview` (server-side `ClassifyTier`) and reused for `/exec`. So capability B's generated command must classify as tier 3. Let me see how the existing tier-3 tools that touch spaced user paths structure their commands:

The key insight: `ClassifyTier` matches `filePatterns` against the **raw** command (quotes included), which is how `mv`-based tier-3 tools work — but there's **no existing tier-3 path for writing file *content*** to the data volume (nas-api writes via `/btrfs/volume1/...`, and the redirect filePatterns only match `/volume\d+/` immediately after the operator). So capability B needs one safe validator addition (a redirect-into-volume pattern that only ever *elevates* classification). Capability A needs none.

  "_tool_descriptions": {
    "check_system_info": "DSM version, NAS model, uptime, CPU, memory — baseline context for any conversation",
    "list_volumes": "Discovers active NAS data volumes and their filesystem usage so later tools do not assume everything lives on /volume1",
    "list_shared_folders": "Lists DSM shared folders with their real paths and selected metadata",
    "inspect_mounts": "Shows the live mount graph for volumes, package storage, and bind mounts",
    "inspect_encryption_state": "Checks whether volumes and share paths appear mounted, unlocked, and visible to packages",
    "check_disk_space": "Shows disk and inode usage for all active data volumes",
    "check_hardware_temps": "CPU/chassis temperature sensors, fan speeds, and SMART disk temperatures",
    "check_volume_health": "DSM-layer RAID/volume health (synovolumestatus, synoarraystatus), mdstat, SMART overall",
    "check_cpu_iowait": "Measures how much time the CPU is stuck waiting on disk — high values mean an I/O problem",
    "check_agent_container": "Confirms the Synology Monitor agent container is running",
    "get_resource_snapshot": "Full picture: top processes, disk activity, network connections, memory, and recent errors — best starting point for any problem",
    "check_io_stalls": "Finds processes stuck waiting on disk, measures disk queue depth, and looks for hung task warnings in the kernel",
    "check_memory_detail": "Full meminfo, swap activity rates, dirty/writeback pages, OOM kill history",
    "check_network_health": "Interface errors/drops, routing table, DNS resolution, listening ports, NFS/SMB connections",
    "check_tailscale": "Tailscale VPN status — interface state, IP, and whether the daemon is reachable",
    "check_network_connections": "Active TCP connections per process, connection state counts, top peers, Drive sync connections",
    "check_security_log": "Failed logins, security events, admin audit entries, SSH connections",
    "check_active_sessions": "Currently active SMB, NFS, SSH, DSM web, and Synology Drive sessions",
    "check_packages": "All installed DSM packages, their running status, and recent package events",
    "tail_system_log": "Recent /var/log/messages — general kernel and service events",
    "tail_drive_server_log": "Shows the most recent Synology Drive server log entries",
    "search_drive_server_log": "Searches Drive server logs for a specific word, share name, or error",
    "tail_sharesync_log": "Shows the most recent ShareSync log entries across all volumes — what files are being synced and any errors",
    "check_sharesync_status": "Looks specifically for stuck, conflicted, or erroring ShareSync tasks across all volumes",
    "check_kernel_io_errors": "Reads the kernel error log for disk errors, SCSI faults, and filesystem corruption",
    "check_share_database": "Lists all shared folders from the DSM database — failures indicate database corruption",
    "check_drive_package_health": "Checks whether the Synology Drive package is properly installed across all volumes",
    "check_drive_database": "Checks Synology Drive's internal databases for corruption across all volumes",
    "search_webapi_log": "Searches DSM WebAPI logs for share access errors and authentication failures",
    "search_all_logs": "Searches every log file on the NAS for a specific word or phrase",
    "find_problematic_files": "Finds files with names that break ShareSync — special characters, conflict files, and extremely long names",

    "check_scheduled_tasks": "Lists all DSM scheduled tasks and their last run result — catches silent cron failures",

    "check_container_io": "Shows which Docker containers are doing the most disk I/O",

    "check_daemon_processes": "PHASE-1B: Shows process state for key Synology daemons: synologand, invoked, syncd, cloud-control, syno_drive_server",
    "inspect_package_lockfiles": "PHASE-1B: Finds stale lock files across all packages and runtime dirs that may prevent package starts",
    "inspect_crash_signals": "PHASE-1B: Looks for crash evidence — segfaults, OOM kills, core dumps, DSM error log events",
    "tail_package_logs": "PHASE-1B: Shows recent log entries for a named DSM package from all known log locations",
    "search_package_logs": "PHASE-1B: Searches all log files for a named DSM package and a filter term",
    "inspect_path_metadata": "PHASE-1E: Shows POSIX metadata for an exact path — owner, group, mode, size, inode, timestamps",
    "inspect_path_acl": "PHASE-1E: Shows POSIX and Synology ACL entries for an exact path via getfacl and synoacltool",
    "inspect_effective_permissions": "PHASE-1E: Shows effective access on a path with optional per-user group membership and share-level access check",
    "check_smart_detail": "PHASE-1C: Full SMART attributes, error log, and self-test history for a disk (all disks or a specific device)",
    "check_scrub_status": "PHASE-1C: Btrfs scrub status for all volumes and RAID sync/check progress from mdstat",
    "check_storage_pool_detail": "PHASE-1C: Detailed storage pool and RAID array state from DSM and mdadm — finds degraded arrays and rebuilding disks",
    "check_btrfs_detail": "PHASE-1C: Btrfs filesystem usage, device error counters, balance status, and subvolume list for all Btrfs volumes",
    "check_disk_error_trends": "PHASE-1C: Compact SMART error table for all disks — reallocated sectors, pending sectors, uncorrectable errors, temperature",
    "check_volume_quota_and_inode_pressure": "PHASE-1C: Inode usage pressure and Btrfs qgroup quota state for all data volumes",
    "check_interface_flaps": "PHASE-1D: Interface carrier change counts, error counters, and link state — detects unstable physical connections",
    "check_bond_health": "PHASE-1D: Bonding/LACP state, slave health, and bond mode if bonded interfaces are configured",
    "check_dns_and_gateway_health": "PHASE-1D: DNS resolution tests, default gateway ping, and nameserver/route configuration",
    "check_service_ports": "PHASE-1D: Listener state and connection count for SMB, NFS, SSH, DSM, Drive sync, LDAP, and rsync ports",
    "check_synology_drive_network": "PHASE-1D: Drive sync port 6690 listener state, connection counts by client, and recent Drive network errors",
    "find_recent_path_changes": "PHASE-1E: Files modified within lookback_hours under an exact path, sorted by mtime",
    "live_file_search": "Strict live filesystem search by filename glob, with no indexing, no fuzzy matching, and no content search. Use for patterns like *budget*.xls* when Universal Search or Windows Search ignore filters.",
    "find_path_versions_and_snapshots": "PHASE-1E: Btrfs snapshots, recycle bin entries, and Drive version hints for an exact path",
    "search_file_access_audit": "PHASE-1E: Searches DSM file access audit logs for a path fragment or username",
    "search_smb_path_activity": "PHASE-1E: Searches Samba/SMB logs for activity related to a path, share, or username",
    "search_drive_path_activity": "PHASE-1E: Searches Drive server and ShareSync logs for activity related to a path or username",
    "hash_file": "PHASE-1E: SHA-256 and MD5 hashes plus timestamps for an exact file path — verify integrity or detect corruption",
    "compare_file_versions": "PHASE-1E: Compares two file paths by metadata, hashes, and optional text diff",
    "collect_incident_bundle": "PHASE-1F: Targeted diagnostic snapshot for an incident type: drive, storage, network, permission, or crash",
    "fetch_log_file": "PHASE-1F: Returns the content of a specific log file path — lists available logs if no path given",
    "fetch_package_db": "PHASE-1F: Queries a named package's SQLite database in read-only mode — lists tables or runs SELECT/WITH/safe PRAGMA queries",
    "fetch_support_artifacts": "PHASE-1F: Lists available DSM support bundles, large log files, package log dirs, and core dumps",
    "run_command": "Run any read-only command the AI needs for deep diagnosis — blocked automatically if it tries to write anything",

content.text: Let me look at the validator test structure so my new cases match, then make all edits:

input.command: cd /tmp/synology-monitor && sed -n '1,60p' apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go
input.description: Read validator_test.go header and first cases

input.command: cd /tmp/synology-monitor && grep -nE 'func Test|ClassifyTier|wantTier|TierFile|TierService' apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go | head -50
input.description: Find tier classification test functions

content.text: Everything checks out. Now making the edits. First the validator — one redirect-into-volume pattern added to **both** `writePatterns` (so the write is detected) and `filePatterns` (so it classifies tier 3); this only ever *elevates* classification:

input.old_string: regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)\bdd\b.*\bof=`),  // dd writing anywhere (device/proc covered by hard-block above)

content.content: The file /tmp/synology-monitor/apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.oldString: regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)\bdd\b.*\bof=`),  // dd writing anywhere (device/proc covered by hard-block above)

lines: regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)\bionice\b.*-c`), // ionice -c sets I/O class; plain ionice -p (read) stays tier 1
regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)\bdd\b.*\bof=`),  // dd writing anywhere (device/proc covered by hard-block above)
+  // Output redirection into a data volume — raw `/volumeN` or the nas-api
+  // `/btrfs/volumeN` writable mount, with or without quotes around the path.
+  // stripQuotedStrings hides a quoted redirect target from hasRealOutputRedirect,
+  // would otherwise read as non-write. This (matched against the raw command)
+  // guarantees it is seen as a write; the twin filePatterns entry makes it tier 3.

lines: +      "WRITE — Raises the Linux inotify limits (fs.inotify.max_user_watches and fs.inotify.max_user_instances) live via sysctl AND persists them to /etc/sysctl.conf so they survive a reboot. The default max_user_watches=8192 is far too low for a containerized seaf-cli worktree with hundreds of thousands of directories: the shared root inotify pool is exhausted, the worktree monitor goes blind, and seaf-cli silently reports 'synchronized' while the worktree has diverged. Pass filter as 'WATCHES' or 'WATCHES INSTANCES' (e.g. '1048576' or '1048576 1024'). Defaults: watches=1048576, instances=1024. Each watch pins ~1 KiB of non-swappable kernel RAM (so 1048576 ≈ up to 1 GiB worst case, only allocated per watch actually held). After running this, restart the seaf-cli daemon so it re-registers watches, then confirm 0 'No space left on device' errors in its log.",
+          "set_inotify_watches: watches must be an integer 8192–4194304. The 4M cap bounds pinned kernel memory (~1 KiB/watch).",
+        throw new Error("set_inotify_watches: instances must be an integer 128–8192.");
+        `echo 'NOTE: restart the seaf-cli daemon so it re-registers watches across the whole worktree.'`,

+      "WRITE (tier 3) — Writes a standard seafile-ignore.txt (the exact filename seaf-cli reads; .seafile-ignore is NOT recognized) into a seaf-cli worktree/library root so the client stops syncing Synology junk: @eaDir thumbnails, #recycle, #snapshot, @tmp, .DS_Store, Thumbs.db, *.tmp. Pass the worktree root in filter, either as a /volume1/... path (auto-mapped to the nas-api /btrfs writable mount) or directly as /btrfs/volume1/.... Known edgesynology1 seaf-cli roots: '/volume1/mac/Art Library', '/volume1/mac/Decor/Character Licensed', '/volume1/mac/Decor/Generic Decor', '/volume1/styleguides'. NOTE: this only stops FUTURE upload of matching files — it does not delete copies already on the Seafile server, and it does not by itself fix inotify watch exhaustion (use set_inotify_watches for that).",
+      if (!p) throw new Error("write_seafile_ignore: filter must be the absolute worktree root path.");
+      if (p.includes("..")) throw new Error("write_seafile_ignore: path must not contain '..'.");
+      // Map to the nas-api writable Btrfs mount; per-share /volumeN mounts are read-only.
+        throw new Error("write_seafile_ignore: path must be under /volume1/ (or /btrfs/volume1/).");
+        `ls -la ${file} 2>/dev/null && echo '(existing ignore file will be OVERWRITTEN)' || echo '(no existing ignore file)'`,

+func TestInotifyAndSeafileIgnoreClassification(t *testing.T) {
+	// set_inotify_watches: live sysctl + persist to /etc/sysctl.conf must stay
+	// tier 2 (service op) — it must NOT be elevated to tier 3 by the new
+	// redirect-into-volume filePattern, because /host/etc is not a data volume.
+	tier2 := []string{
+		"sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_watches=1048576 2>&1",
+		"sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_instances=1024 2>&1",
+		`echo "fs.inotify.max_user_watches=1048576" >> /host/etc/sysctl.conf`,
+		`sed -i '/fs\.inotify\.max_user_watches/d;/fs\.inotify\.max_user_instances/d' /host/etc/sysctl.conf 2>&1`,
+	}
+	for _, cmd := range tier2 {
+		if got := ClassifyTier(cmd); got != TierService {
+			t.Fatalf("ClassifyTier(%q) = %d, want TierService(2)", cmd, got)
+		}
+		if err := Validate(cmd, TierService); err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("Validate(TierService) rejected %q: %v", cmd, err)
+		}
+		if err := Validate(cmd, TierRead); err == nil {
+			t.Fatalf("Validate(TierRead) unexpectedly allowed write %q", cmd)
+		}
+	}
+
+	// write_seafile_ignore: a content write into the /btrfs/volumeN writable mount
+	// (quoted path with a space) must classify as tier 3 and validate at tier 3.
+	tier3 := []string{
+		`printf '%s\n' '@eaDir' '#recycle' > '/btrfs/volume1/mac/Decor/Generic Decor/seafile-ignore.txt'`,
+		`printf '%s\n' '@eaDir' >> '/volume1/styleguides/seafile-ignore.txt'`,
+	}
+	for _, cmd := range tier3 {
+		if got := ClassifyTier(cmd); got != TierFile {
+			t.Fatalf("ClassifyTier(%q) = %d, want TierFile(3)", cmd, got)
+		}
+		if err := Validate(cmd, TierFile); err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("Validate(TierFile) rejected %q: %v", cmd, err)
+		}
+		// A content write to a data volume must never be allowed at tier 1 or 2.
+		if err := Validate(cmd, TierRead); err == nil {
+			t.Fatalf("Validate(TierRead) unexpectedly allowed volume write %q", cmd)
+		}
+		if err := Validate(cmd, TierService); err == nil {
+			t.Fatalf("Validate(TierService) unexpectedly allowed volume write %q", cmd)
+		}
+	}
+}

input.old_string:
## Tooling constraint
nas-direct MCP has NO capability to set inotify sysctl, write arbitrary files, or create boot tasks (`write_tasks` only runs/enables/disables existing tasks). `run_command` blocks writes. Apply via the documented base64 run_command bypass (see CONTEXT_FOR_AI.md), via DSM directly, or by adding a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api. edgesynology1 is the source of truth (NAS always wins), so conflict direction is fixed.
input.new_string:
## Tooling constraint → resolved by new capabilities
Originally nas-direct MCP had NO capability to set inotify sysctl or write files. Two new MCP capabilities were added to the `u2giants/synology-monitor` repo (2026-06-19): **`set_inotify_watches`** (live `sysctl -w` + persist to `/host/etc/sysctl.conf`, tier 2) and **`write_seafile_ignore`** (writes `seafile-ignore.txt` into a seaf-cli worktree root via the `/btrfs/volume1` writable mount, tier 3). Files changed: `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` (defs + TOOL_GROUPS), `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` (enabled_write_tools), `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` (one redirect-into-volume pattern added to writePatterns+filePatterns so content writes to `/btrfs/volumeN` classify tier 3) + `validator_test.go`. Go validator tests + TS type-check pass. **Pending: commit/push to `main`** → CI builds images → Watchtower updates nas-api (~5 min) + Coolify redeploys nas-mcp. No compose change needed (`/host/etc` already mounted, per `persist_vm_overcommit_memory`). seaf-cli worktree→host map (mountinfo): art=`/volume1/mac/Art Library`, char=`/volume1/mac/Decor/Character Licensed`, decor=`/volume1/mac/Decor/Generic Decor`, guides=`/volume1/styleguides`. edgesynology1 is source of truth (NAS always wins). Cloned working copy was /tmp/synology-monitor (ephemeral); canonical local copy per repo is /worksp/monitor/app.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: seafile-sync-false-synchronized-root-cause-inotify-watch-exhaustion
description: "Why seaf-cli on edgesynology1 silently reports \"synchronized\" while diverging — inotify max_user_watches=8192 starves the worktree monitor"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 8e5209fb-6aac-4cff-aba5-9813fd333a2f
---

On 2026-06-19, Generic Decor (under the `decor` library) drifted: seaf-cli reported **synchronized** while the NAS worktree and the server head disagreed. A restart "fixed" it, but that only masked the real bug. Related: [[Seafile Pro Deployment — designflow.app]].

## Root cause — the NAS watcher went blind (NOT a stale/corrupt index)
The `seaf-cli` client runs in the `seaf-cli` Docker container on edgesynology1 (`seaf-daemon`, config `-c /root/.ccnet`, worktree libraries mounted at `/library/{art,char,decor,guides}`). Its log `/root/.ccnet/logs/seafile.log` contains, **11,077 times**:
`wt-monitor-linux.c(542): [wt mon] fail to add watch to /library/decor: No space left on device`

"No space left on device" here is **NOT disk** (disk 52% used, 53 TB free; clocks NAS↔server within ~20s). It is the **Linux inotify watch limit**: `fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 8192` (the low default). This is a per-UID pool shared by ALL root-owned processes on the host (DSM services + every container). seaf-daemon could only register **3,209 watches** before the shared pool was exhausted, then every further directory — including `/library/decor`, which contains Generic Decor — got **no watch**.

**Mechanism:** seaf-daemon only re-indexes/commits a library when its inotify monitor fires a change event for it. An unwatched directory never fires → edits there are never detected → the daemon believes nothing changed → `seaf-cli status` honestly reports "synchronized" while the worktree has diverged. That is the false-synchronized state.

**Why the restart only masked it:** startup runs a one-time full re-index that caught the drift once, but the watch errors are timestamped *during that same restart* — the daemon is blind again immediately. It WILL recur on any library past the watch ceiling. A watchdog that "detect drift → restart daemon" would hide this permanently (each restart's full scan resets the symptom; root cause persists).

## Measurements (2026-06-19)
Worktree directory counts (`find -type d`):
- art 83,851 (80,131 inside `@eaDir`) · char 377,816 (319,996) · decor 28,103 (22,220) · guides 51,321 (22,352)
- **Total ≈ 541,000 dirs; ~444,700 (82%) are Synology `@eaDir` thumbnail folders.** Real content dirs ≈ 96,400.
- seaf-daemon held 3,209 watches; host RAM 32 GB.

## Fix
1. **Raise `fs.inotify.max_user_watches` to 1,048,576** (covers full 541k worktree + growth even if ignore doesn't reduce watches). `max_user_instances` → 1024. ~1 KiB pinned kernel mem per watch used (≈96 MB at real load; ≤1 GB worst case of 32 GB). 400k is too low — below the current 541k dir count, so it would NOT fix the bug unless @eaDir is also excluded.
2. **Persist across reboot.** On this host `/etc/sysctl.conf` IS honored (the nas-api `persist_vm_overcommit_memory` capability writes there). Add the inotify line there, or a DSM boot-up Task Scheduler task running `sysctl -w`.
3. **Exclude junk** via `seafile-ignore.txt` in each library root (`@eaDir`, `#recycle`, `#snapshot`, `@tmp`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `*.tmp`) — cuts dirs ~82%, reduces server junk + scan time. **Verified 2026-06-19: NO ignore files exist on disk by any name** (a prior session claimed it created `.seafile-ignore` files but none are present — and the name is wrong anyway). The file MUST be named exactly `seafile-ignore.txt` at each library root; `.seafile-ignore` is not recognized by Seafile. Caveats: adding ignore does NOT delete already-synced server copies (remove separately); Synology constantly regenerates `@eaDir` locally so they reappear (ignore only stops re-upload); uncertain whether the wt-monitor skips watches for ignored dirs, so do NOT rely on it for the watch fix — the limit raise is the guaranteed fix.
4. **Restart seaf-daemon**, then verify: `grep -c 'No space left on device'` for entries after the restart must be **0**.

## Tooling constraint
nas-direct MCP has NO capability to set inotify sysctl, write arbitrary files, or create boot tasks (`write_tasks` only runs/enables/disables existing tasks). `run_command` blocks writes. Apply via the documented base64 run_command bypass (see CONTEXT_FOR_AI.md), via DSM directly, or by adding a `set_inotify_watches` capability to nas-api. edgesynology1 is the source of truth (NAS always wins), so conflict direction is fixed.

The file MUST be named exactly `seafile-ignore.txt` at each library root; `.seafile-ignore` is not recognized by Seafile. content.text:
Done. Here's what I did and where it stands.

## Read the docs first (filtered to nas-api)
The governing files told me capabilities aren't bespoke code — they're **tool defs in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`** that emit shell commands, tier-classified by **`apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`** and enabled in **`apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`**. There was a near-exact precedent (`set_vm_overcommit_memory` + `persist_vm_overcommit_memory`) doing `sysctl -w` plus a `/etc/sysctl.conf` write. I discarded the web/pipeline/Supabase/agent-collector material as irrelevant.

## Two capabilities added (4 files, +140 lines)

**`set_inotify_watches`** (tier 2) — live `sysctl -w` of `max_user_watches`/`max_user_instances` + persist to `/host/etc/sysctl.conf`. Defaults 1048576 / 1024, bounds-checked (8192–4194304 watches, the 4M cap bounding pinned kernel RAM). Mirrors the vm.overcommit precedent exactly → **no validator change needed**.

**`write_seafile_ignore`** (tier 3) — writes a correctly-named `seafile-ignore.txt` (the fixed junk list) into a seaf-cli worktree root, auto-mapping `/volume1/...` → the writable `/btrfs/volume1` mount. The description hardcodes the 4 discovered roots (e.g. `/volume1/mac/Decor/Generic Decor`).

**One validator addition** was genuinely required: nas-api writes user data via `/btrfs/volumeN`, but the existing redirect file-patterns only matched `/volumeN` *immediately* after the operator, and `stripQuotedStrings` hid quoted spaced paths. I added `(>>?)\s*['"]?/(btrfs/)?volume\d+/` to **both** `writePatterns` (so the write is detected) and `filePatterns` (so it lands at tier 3). It only ever *elevates* classification — safe by construction.

## Verified
- **Go validator tests: `ok`** — new test asserts the inotify commands stay tier 2 (not wrongly elevated by the new pattern, since `/host/etc` isn't a volume) and the seafile-ignore writes classify tier 3 and are rejected at tiers 1/2.
- **TS type-check + shared build: clean** across packages.
- Reverted the regenerated `tsconfig.tsbuildinfo` artifact; diff is just the 4 real files.

## Not done (needs your go-ahead)
Per the repo's `AI_OPERATING_RULES` (single `main` branch, commit only when explicitly asked), I have **not committed or pushed**. The deploy path once you approve: commit → push to `main` → CI builds → Watchtower updates **nas-api** (~5 min) + Coolify redeploys **nas-mcp**. No compose change needed (`/host/etc` is already mounted). Note I worked in an ephemeral clone at `/tmp/synology-monitor`; the repo's canonical local copy is `/worksp/monitor/app`.

Want me to commit and push to `main` (I'll write the commit message in their `area: imperative` style), or would you rather review/apply the diff in your `/worksp/monitor/app` working copy yourself?

content: i'm going to remove all the @eaDir directories. in that case should we reduce max_user_watches to 200,000 ?

message.content: i'm going to remove all the @eaDir directories. in that case should we reduce max_user_watches to 200,000 ?

content.text:
Short answer: **no — leave it high (1,048,576, or 524,288 at the very least). Don't drop to 200k.**

The reasoning hinges on one fact that's easy to miss: **`max_user_watches` is a ceiling, not an allocation.** The kernel only pins ~1 KiB per watch *actually registered*, not per the limit. So after you delete `@eaDir` and the worktree settles at ~96,400 dirs, the daemon registers ~96k watches ≈ **~96 MB whether the ceiling is 200k or 1M.** Lowering the ceiling saves you **zero** real memory — it only removes headroom.

Given that, the trade is entirely one-sided:

- **Downside of a high ceiling:** essentially nothing (no extra memory until watches are held; worst-case theoretical pin on a 32 GB box is a non-issue).
- **Downside of a low ceiling:** if watches ever exhaust again, the daemon silently goes blind and falsely reports "synchronized" — the exact bug we just fixed. Silent and catastrophic.

And there are three concrete ways 200k gets eaten:

1. **`@eaDir` regenerates.** Synology recreates those thumbnail folders via Media/Universal Search indexing. `seafile-ignore.txt` stops them *syncing*, but not their *creation on disk* — and the watcher likely watches on-disk dirs regardless of ignore. So your ~96k creeps back toward 541k unless you also **disable thumbnail/media indexing on those shares** (Control Panel → Indexing Service / disable thumbnail gen). If you don't, 200k is exceeded again fairly quickly.
2. **The pool is shared.** `max_user_watches` is per-UID across *all* root processes on the host (DSM services + every container), not just seaf-daemon. Other consumers eat into it.
3. **Libraries grow** over time.

So: 200k = ~2× headroom that erodes; 1M = ~10× headroom that costs nothing extra. Easy call.

**What I'd do:**
- Keep `max_user_watches = 1048576` (the value already in the new `set_inotify_watches` capability).
- Delete `@eaDir` — it's worth doing for its own sake (less synced junk, faster scans, less server storage), just **not** as a reason to lower the ceiling.
- Also disable DSM thumbnail/indexing on those shares so `@eaDir` doesn't silently rebuild — otherwise the cleanup is temporary.

If you specifically want to reclaim memory, the lever is fewer *actual* watches (deleting `@eaDir` + keeping it gone), which you're already doing — the ceiling number doesn't affect that.

content.text:
Good question on (1), and then I'll do the documentation pass. First, the answer:

## Why disabling DSM indexing is a *separate* layer from `seafile-ignore.txt`

They operate at two different layers, and the gap between them is the open question:

- **`seafile-ignore.txt` = Seafile sync layer.** It tells `seaf-cli` not to *upload* matching files to the server. It does **not** touch the NAS filesystem — DSM still creates `@eaDir` folders on disk via Media Indexing / thumbnail generation.
- **DSM indexing = filesystem layer.** It's what *creates* `@eaDir` on disk in the first place.

The thing that exhausts inotify is **on-disk directories the worktree monitor watches** — and the unverified question is: **does `seaf-cli`'s monitor add inotify watches to directories that are listed in `seafile-ignore.txt`?**

- If the monitor **honors** ignore when registering watches → `seafile-ignore.txt` alone keeps the watch count down, and you do **not** need to disable indexing for the watch problem. `@eaDir` regrows on disk but consumes no watches.
- If the monitor **watches everything** and applies ignore only at commit time (common in older Seafile clients) → `seafile-ignore.txt` does **nothing** for watch count, `@eaDir` regrowth re-exhausts, and you'd need either a high ceiling or indexing disabled.

I don't know which behavior this client has — so my "disable indexing" suggestion was a hedge against the second case. **But here's the key point that resolves it:** because we're keeping the ceiling at ~1M (10× headroom), **you don't actually need to disable indexing at all.** The high ceiling already absorbs `@eaDir` regrowth regardless of which behavior is true. Disabling indexing only becomes necessary if you insist on a *low* ceiling (like the 200k you asked about) — which is exactly why I tied them together.

**To verify which behavior you have** (worth doing once `seafile-ignore.txt` is deployed + daemon restarted): count the daemon's held watches (`grep -c '^inotify wd:' /proc/<pid>/fdinfo/<inotify-fd>`) and check whether `fail to add watch … @eaDir` errors persist. If watches stay high / `@eaDir` still errors → the monitor ignores the ignore file for watching.

So: with the ceiling high, **`seafile-ignore.txt` is sufficient for hygiene and you can skip disabling indexing.** Now the documentation.

| Change database schema, migrations, models, external IDs, or data flow | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env/config is affected, relevant migration/model files | Deployment docs unless rollout/deploy behavior changes |
| Investigate bugs or incidents | `AGENTS.md`, relevant docs based on affected area, `HANDOFF.md` if present, Critical incidents section in `AGENTS.md`, relevant incident docs under `docs/` | Unrelated folder-level READMEs |
| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md`, relevant docs named inside `HANDOFF.md` | Docs unrelated to the handoff scope |
| Work on the archive feature (file inventory or archive move) | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/synology-archive.md` (design/behavior), `docs/synology-archive-implementation.md` (build guide), `docs/archive-move-runbook.md` (live-move operator steps), `docs/architecture.md` (nas-api job API), `docs/deployment.md` (on-NAS job state + snapshots) | Unrelated subsystem docs |
| Work in a subfolder with its own README | `AGENTS.md`, that folder-level `README.md`, and only broader docs referenced there | Other folder-level READMEs |
| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | Other docs unless task requires them |
| Documentation-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected docs under `docs/`, folder-level READMEs only where relevant | Source files except as needed to verify accuracy |

This map is intentionally task-based. Do not load every Markdown file by default.
